proposals.title: Distinguishing Commensal from Pathogenic Candida Subspecies in Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Longitudinal Microbiome and Host-Factor Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current paradigms in recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) often treat all Candida isolates, particularly non-albicans species, as inherently pathogenic. This may lead to overtreatment and obscure the true pathophysiology. Pre-2026 research increasingly recognizes the vaginal microbiome as a complex ecosystem, yet the assumption of universal Candida pathogenicity remains largely untested, especially regarding subspecies variation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within Candida species (e.g., C. glabrata, C. parapsilosis), specific genetic subspecies or strains are commensal residents of the vaginal microbiota and only cause symptomatic infection when host defenses are compromised, rather than due to intrinsic virulence factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month longitudinal cohort study of 200 women with a history of RVVC and 100 control women without RVVC. Vaginal swabs will be collected monthly and during symptomatic episodes. We will perform whole-genome sequencing on all Candida isolates to delineate subspecies/strain-level phylogeny. Concurrently, we will measure host compromise markers (e.g., local cytokine panels, beta-defensin levels, glycogen content) and characterize the bacterial microbiome via 16S rRNA sequencing. Statistical models will correlate specific Candida strains with symptomatic episodes, controlling for host markers and bacterial community state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, strain-level framework for understanding Candida pathogenicity in RVVC, moving beyond species-level associations. It could identify specific commensal strains, leading to more precise diagnostic criteria that differentiate colonization from true infection. The findings may inform selective treatment strategies and novel prevention approaches targeting host factors rather than commensal eradication.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on azole resistance and species prevalence, this proposal directly challenges the core assumption of universal pathogenicity by integrating high-resolution fungal genomics with longitudinal host-factor data to define pathogenicity at the subspecies level.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Long-Term Cohort Study to Quantify the Risk of Colorectal Neoplasia Following Streptococcus bovis Group Bacteremia
proposals.motivation: A well-established but poorly quantified association exists between Streptococcus bovis group (S. gallolyticus) bacteremia and occult colorectal neoplasia, including cancer. Current guidelines recommend colonoscopy for these patients, but the long-term, longitudinal risk after the initial diagnostic work-up remains unclear. Defining this risk is critical for evidence-based recommendations on surveillance intervals and patient counseling.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients diagnosed with S. bovis group bacteremia have a significantly higher long-term incidence of new or recurrent colorectal neoplasia compared to a matched control population, necessitating extended endoscopic surveillance beyond the initial diagnosis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study with prospective follow-up. Adult patients with a first episode of S. bovis group bacteremia (index cohort) from 2015-2020 will be identified via microbiology databases. A control cohort matched for age, sex, and comorbidities will be assembled from patients with E. coli bacteremia. Primary outcome is the incidence of advanced colorectal neoplasia (adenocarcinoma or advanced adenoma) diagnosed >12 months after bacteremia. Data on colonoscopy findings, pathology, and demographics will be collected. Time-to-event analysis (Kaplan-Meier, Cox proportional hazards) will compare cancer-free survival between cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal data quantifying the long-term neoplastic risk in this patient population. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on the necessity and optimal timing of follow-up colonoscopies after the initial post-bacteremia evaluation, moving beyond anecdotal association to evidence-based surveillance strategy.
proposals.novelty: While the link is known, prior studies are largely cross-sectional at diagnosis. This proposal focuses on the novel question of long-term risk *after* the initial diagnostic colonoscopy, a critical gap in management. The use of a contemporaneous bacteremia control group (E. coli) strengthens causal inference beyond general population comparisons.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Characterization of Bacterial Innate Immune Pathways Against Engineered Phage DNA
proposals.motivation: The rise of antibiotic-resistant pathogens, including azole-resistant Candida species, necessitates novel therapeutic strategies. Phage therapy and CRISPR-based antimicrobials represent promising alternatives, but their efficacy is threatened by bacterial defense systems that recognize and inactivate foreign genetic material. Understanding these bacterial immune mechanisms is critical to designing next-generation antimicrobials that can overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bacteria possess specific, genetically encoded restriction-modification-like systems or novel CRISPR-Cas-independent pathways that detect and degrade phage DNA containing synthetic modifications (e.g., methylated or hydroxymethylated bases) used to evade canonical defenses.
proposals.method: We will employ a functional genomics screen using a comprehensive transposon mutant library of Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Mutants will be challenged with a panel of T7 phages engineered to contain defined DNA modifications (e.g., 5-methylcytosine, 5-hydroxymethylcytosine). Survival assays will identify mutant strains with increased susceptibility, pinpointing genes essential for defense. Candidate genes will be cloned and their biochemical activity characterized in vitro via electrophoretic mobility shift assays (EMSAs) and nuclease activity tests on modified DNA substrates.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the genetic landscape of bacterial immunity against engineered phages, identifying new resistance determinants. It will provide a foundational knowledge base for predicting and countering bacterial evasion of advanced phage therapies, directly informing the design of more resilient antimicrobial agents.
proposals.novelty: While bacterial defenses against natural phages are well-studied, systematic investigation of responses to phages with synthetically modified DNA—a key strategy in therapeutic phage engineering—remains unexplored. This proposal shifts the focus from natural co-evolution to engineered confrontation.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Assess the Immunogenicity of a Single Site-Specific Methylated Cytosine in a Synthetic Bacterial DNA Sequence
proposals.motivation: Bacterial immune systems, such as restriction-modification (R-M) systems, recognize and cleave foreign DNA based on methylation patterns. Prior work demonstrates that bacterial defense can target broadly modified DNA, but the minimal unit of recognition—whether a single, specific methylated base is sufficient—remains untested. Defining this threshold is crucial for understanding fundamental immune evasion mechanisms in pathogens like Candida species, where epigenetic regulation may influence host-microbe interactions and antifungal resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the introduction of a single, site-specific methylated cytosine (e.g., 5-methylcytosine) within a synthetic oligonucleotide will be sufficient to trigger a measurable immune rejection response in a model bacterial system with a cognate restriction endonuclease, compared to an unmethylated control.
proposals.method: We will synthesize identical, fluorescently labeled double-stranded DNA oligonucleotides, with the experimental arm containing a single 5-methylcytosine at a predetermined site within the recognition sequence of the commercially available restriction enzyme DpnI (which cuts methylated GATC). Using an in vitro bacterial lysate assay from E. coli strains engineered to express DpnI or a control, we will measure immune rejection via gel electrophoresis quantification of cleaved vs. intact DNA. The primary endpoint will be the cleavage efficiency, analyzed by a blinded reviewer comparing the methylated oligonucleotide to the unmethylated (placebo) oligonucleotide, with statistical significance determined by a two-tailed t-test.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the minimal epigenetic signal required to activate a bacterial innate immune response, providing a foundational metric for bacterial immunology. It will directly inform the development of novel antimicrobial strategies that exploit or evade DNA modification-based recognition, with potential translational relevance for managing infections involving drug-resistant organisms.
proposals.novelty: While R-M systems are well-characterized, no prior study has isolated and tested the sufficiency of a single, site-specific methylation event as an immunogenic trigger in a controlled, placebo-like experimental design, moving from population-level patterns to a definitive atomic-scale determinant.

proposals.title: Identification of Host Immunologic and Comorbidity Factors Predictive of Extended Antifungal Therapy Duration in Women with Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Secondary Analysis of the Ibrexafungerp Phase 3 Trial Cohort
proposals.motivation: The clinical classification of 'abnormal host' in recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) is poorly defined, leading to inconsistent treatment strategies. Identifying specific host factors that predict a need for longer-duration therapy would enable personalized, more effective management. This is critical as azole resistance becomes more prevalent, necessitating optimized use of newer agents like ibrexafungerp.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific host factors, including subtle immune dysregulation (e.g., cytokine profiles, monocyte function) and specific comorbidities (e.g., poorly controlled diabetes, specific vaginal microbiota states), will be significantly associated with treatment failure following standard short-course therapy and will predict the need for extended antifungal regimens.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of clinical and biospecimen data from the Phase 3 ibrexafungerp trial cohort. Host factors will be systematically captured from baseline records: comorbidities, medication use, and vaginal microbiome data (16S rRNA sequencing). Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from a subset of participants will be analyzed via multiplex cytokine assays (Luminex) and functional immune assays (e.g., Candida-stimulated cytokine production). A multivariate logistic regression model will be developed to identify factors associated with recurrence within 6 months post-treatment, with model performance validated using bootstrapping techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven definition of 'abnormal host' in RVVC, moving beyond clinical gestalt. It will generate a predictive model to stratify patients for standard versus extended-duration therapy, directly informing clinical trial design and treatment guidelines. The findings will offer mechanistic insights into host-pathogen interactions in RVVC treatment failure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links broad conditions like diabetes to RVVC risk, this study uniquely integrates detailed immunologic profiling with clinical and microbiome data from a rigorously characterized trial cohort to create a predictive model for therapeutic duration, a novel clinical endpoint.

proposals.title: A Clinical Decision Tool for Personalized Antifungal Therapy Duration in Acute Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Pilot Study Integrating Host Risk Factors and Pathogen Characteristics
proposals.motivation: Current treatment for acute vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) uses fixed-duration regimens, which may lead to undertreatment in complex cases or overtreatment in simple ones, potentially driving resistance and recurrence. Antimicrobial stewardship principles, successful in bacterial infections, have not been systematically applied to fungal VVC therapy. A tool to personalize therapy duration based on individual risk could optimize outcomes and antifungal use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a diagnostic algorithm incorporating specific host factors (e.g., diabetes, recent antibiotic use, contraceptive method) and pathogen factors (e.g., Candida species, in vitro fluconazole susceptibility) can accurately identify women with acute VVC who require extended-duration antifungal therapy (>7 days) versus those who will respond to standard short-course therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 300 women with culture-confirmed acute VVC. We will collect baseline host data and Candida isolate characteristics. Using pre-2026 machine learning methods (specifically, a Random Forest classifier), we will develop an algorithm to predict clinical cure at the 30-day follow-up based on these inputs. The model will be trained to output a recommended therapy duration (standard vs. extended). Its performance will be validated against actual outcomes from a subset of participants treated with a standardized fluconazole regimen.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first prototype clinical decision tool for personalized antifungal therapy duration in VVC, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches. It will provide a framework for antifungal stewardship in gynecology and generate hypothesis-driven data on risk factors for short-term treatment failure.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sobel's prior work has tested new drugs (e.g., ibrexafungerp) and partner treatment, this study innovatively applies diagnostic and stewardship algorithms—common in bacteriology—to the management of a fungal infection to optimize an existing, widely used drug (fluconazole).

proposals.title: Population-Level Impact and Cost-Effectiveness of a Two-Dose Antifungal Regimen for Severe Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Decision-Analytic Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Severe vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) causes significant morbidity and healthcare burden. Current single-dose azole therapy, while effective for mild-moderate cases, has suboptimal efficacy in severe VVC, leading to persistent symptoms, repeat visits, and potential development of resistance. A two-dose regimen (e.g., fluconazole 150 mg doses 72 hours apart) is clinically recognized but not universally adopted in guidelines; its population-level public health impact and economic value remain unquantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a two-dose antifungal regimen as first-line therapy for severe VVC will be cost-effective from a US healthcare payer perspective and will significantly reduce the population-level burden of treatment failure and recurrent infection compared to the standard single-dose regimen.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-transition (Markov) microsimulation model comparing single-dose vs. two-dose fluconazole for severe VVC in a hypothetical cohort of 100,000 women. Model parameters (efficacy, recurrence rates, adverse events, costs, utilities) will be derived from published RCTs (including Dr. Sobel's work), meta-analyses, and US healthcare databases. We will use a Monte Carlo simulation with 10,000 iterations to account for parameter uncertainty. The primary outcomes are the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) in dollars per quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) gained and the number of severe VVC cases and recurrences averted over a one-year time horizon.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive model quantifying the public health and economic consequences of a two-dose strategy for severe VVC. It will deliver actionable evidence for guideline committees (e.g., CDC, IDSA) by defining the cost-effectiveness threshold. The model will also serve as a framework for evaluating newer antifungal agents (e.g., ibrexafungerp) in severe and recurrent VVC.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sobel's prior trials established the clinical efficacy of specific therapies, this study innovatively applies decision-science modeling to translate those individual-patient results into population-level policy recommendations, explicitly addressing the cost-effectiveness gap for a common but under-modeled treatment strategy.

proposals.title: A Phase 2, Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial of a Two-Dose Oral Ibrexafungerp Regimen for the Prevention of Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis in High-Risk Women
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC), defined as ≥3 episodes per year, significantly impairs quality of life. While long-term maintenance fluconazole is effective, it carries risks of resistance and is not suitable for all patients. Ibrexafungerp, a novel oral triterpenoid antifungal with a distinct mechanism, has shown efficacy in treating acute VVC and reducing recurrence in a monthly regimen, but its potential as a short-course, pre-emptive prophylaxis in high-risk patients remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-dose oral ibrexafungerp regimen, administered at the onset of high-risk conditions (e.g., antibiotic use, luteal phase in susceptible women), will be superior to placebo in preventing the development of acute vulvovaginal candidiasis episodes over a 6-month follow-up period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. High-risk participants (women with a documented history of RVVC but currently asymptomatic) will be randomized 1:1 to receive either two oral doses of ibrexafungerp (e.g., 300 mg, 12 hours apart) or matching placebo, to be self-initiated at the start of a predefined high-risk trigger (initiation of systemic antibiotics or the first day of the luteal phase). The primary endpoint will be the proportion of participants developing a culture-confirmed, symptomatic acute VVC episode within 6 months. Secondary endpoints include time to first recurrence, safety, and quality-of-life assessments.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first efficacy data for a short-course, on-demand prophylactic strategy using ibrexafungerp for RVVC. It could establish a new, convenient, and potentially resistance-sparing paradigm for managing recurrence risk, moving from chronic suppression to targeted, event-driven prevention. The findings will directly inform clinical practice for a patient population with significant unmet need.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the established use of ibrexafungerp from treating active infection or providing chronic suppression to testing a novel, minimalist prophylactic dosing strategy triggered by patient-identified risk factors. It directly tests the 'inverse problem' of prophylaxis using a non-azole agent in a precise, event-driven manner not previously studied.

proposals.title: Prevalence and Antifungal Susceptibility of Non-albicans Candida Species in Symptomatic Vulvovaginal Candidiasis During Pregnancy: A Prospective, Multicenter Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is highly prevalent in pregnancy, with Candida albicans considered the dominant pathogen. However, non-albicans species like C. glabrata and C. krusei are of increasing concern due to intrinsic or acquired azole resistance, which complicates treatment. The true prevalence of these species in pregnant populations is poorly characterized, potentially leading to suboptimal empirical therapy and poor outcomes in this vulnerable group.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prevalence of non-albicans Candida species, particularly C. glabrata and C. krusei, in pregnant women with symptomatic VVC is significantly higher than the commonly cited rates from non-pregnant populations, and that a substantial proportion of these isolates exhibit reduced susceptibility to fluconazole.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study across 8-10 obstetric clinics. Pregnant women (n=600) with clinical symptoms of VVC will be enrolled. Vaginal swabs will be cultured on chromogenic agar for presumptive species identification, confirmed by MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry. All isolates will undergo antifungal susceptibility testing (AFST) against fluconazole, ibrexafungerp, and other relevant antifungals using CLSI broth microdilution methods. Demographic and clinical data will be collected, and statistical analysis (chi-square, logistic regression) will compare species prevalence and resistance profiles to historical non-pregnant controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, contemporary data on the epidemiology of VVC-causing species in pregnancy, directly informing empirical treatment guidelines. It will clarify the risk of azole-resistant infections in this population and generate crucial susceptibility data for newer agents like ibrexafungerp, guiding future therapeutic trials.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have focused on C. albicans in pregnancy, this proposal systematically investigates the understudied burden of non-albicans species with concurrent AFST, a combination not extensively explored pre-2026. It directly tests the clinical assumption that these species are 'rare' in pregnancy.

proposals.title: Genetic and Phenotypic Characterization of Flucytosine Resistance in Candida glabrata and Candida krusei Isolates from Pregnant Women with Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Flucytosine is a cornerstone antifungal for severe candidiasis, but intrinsic resistance in C. glabrata and C. krusei is a major clinical concern, particularly in pregnancy where treatment options are limited. The genetic and phenotypic mechanisms underlying this high resistance in these species are not fully elucidated, hindering the development of diagnostic tools and novel therapeutic strategies for this vulnerable patient population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the high flucytosine resistance in C. glabrata and C. krusei is driven by specific, conserved mutations in the FCY2 (cytosine permease) and FUR1 (uracil phosphoribosyltransferase) genes, and that these genetic alterations correlate with distinct in vitro phenotypic profiles under conditions mimicking the vaginal microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will conduct a laboratory-based study on a biobank of clinically confirmed C. glabrata and C. krusei isolates from pregnant women with VVC. Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) to flucytosine will be determined using CLSI broth microdilution. Whole-genome sequencing (Illumina NovaSeq) will be performed on all isolates, with bioinformatic analysis (using BWA/GATK pipelines) focused on variants in FCY2, FUR1, FCY21, and FCY22. Phenotypic assays will assess growth kinetics and cytosine uptake in media adjusted to vaginal pH (4.5).
proposals.contribution: This work will define the primary genetic determinants of flucytosine resistance in these key non-albicans Candida species within a clinically relevant context. It will provide a foundational dataset linking genotype to phenotype, which can inform future rapid molecular diagnostics. The findings will guide clinical decision-making and highlight potential targets for adjunctive therapies to overcome this resistance.
proposals.novelty: While flucytosine resistance mechanisms are studied in general isolates, this proposal uniquely focuses on isolates from pregnant women, a high-risk group, and integrates genetic analysis with phenotypic testing under physiologically relevant vaginal conditions, which is not standard in prior resistance studies.

proposals.title: Association of Non-albicans Candida Species with Symptom Severity and Treatment Outcomes in Acute and Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The epidemiology of vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is shifting, with increasing prevalence of non-albicans Candida (NAC) species like C. glabrata and C. krusei, which often exhibit reduced azole susceptibility. Prior to 2026, the clinical implications of this shift remain poorly characterized, particularly whether infections with these species lead to more severe symptoms or higher rates of treatment failure compared to C. albicans. Clarifying this is critical for guiding empirical therapy and the development of new antifungals like ibrexafungerp.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vulvovaginal candidiasis caused by C. glabrata or C. krusei is associated with more severe patient-reported symptom scores and a higher rate of clinical and mycological treatment failure following standard azole therapy compared to infection with C. albicans.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 400 women with culture-confirmed VVC. Participants will undergo baseline clinical assessment using a standardized symptom severity score (VVC-SSS) and vaginal culture with MALDI-TOF MS for species identification. All participants will receive a standard course of oral fluconazole (150mg) and be followed at days 7 and 30 for clinical cure assessment and repeat culture. The primary analysis will use multivariable logistic regression to model the association between Candida species (C. albicans vs. NAC species) and the composite outcome of treatment failure, adjusting for confounders like history of RVVC. Secondary analysis will compare mean symptom scores by species using ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, prospective data on the clinical phenotype of NAC VVC, directly linking species to patient-centered outcomes. It will inform clinical guidelines on the need for species identification in severe or refractory cases and provide a evidence base for the targeted use of newer non-azole therapies in specific microbiological contexts.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe NAC prevalence, few prospectively correlate specific species with quantified symptom severity and treatment outcomes in a standardized treatment cohort, and none have done so in the context of emerging oral therapies like ibrexafungerp.

proposals.title: Genomic Epidemiology of Candida albicans in an Antenatal Clinic: Tracking Strain Transmission and Azole Resistance Evolution
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) is a significant burden for many women, and its management is complicated by rising azole resistance. Antenatal clinics represent a high-risk setting where physiological changes increase susceptibility, yet the patterns of Candida strain transmission and the in-host evolution of resistance during routine care are poorly understood. Applying the established tools of bacterial genomic epidemiology to Candida could reveal critical reservoirs and transmission dynamics, informing targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that whole-genome sequencing (WGS) of serial Candida isolates from pregnant patients will reveal discrete transmission clusters within the antenatal clinic and identify specific genetic mutations associated with the de novo emergence of fluconazole resistance during follow-up.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study in a large antenatal clinic, collecting vaginal swabs from consenting pregnant women at their first and third trimester visits. Candida isolates will undergo whole-genome sequencing on the Illumina platform. Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) will be called against the Candida albicans SC5314 reference genome. Phylogenetic analysis using maximum-likelihood methods (e.g., RAxML) and transmission network inference (using tools like PHYLOViZ) will identify related strain clusters. For isolates from patients developing clinical resistance or microbiological failure on fluconazole, we will perform variant calling to identify mutations in ERG11 and other genes linked to azole resistance, correlating these with antifungal susceptibility testing (CLSI M27).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution map of Candida transmission in a clinical obstetric setting, defining the proportion of RVVC cases attributable to new acquisition versus persistent strain evolution. It will directly link specific genetic mutations to the clinical failure of first-line azole therapy in a prospective, longitudinal design, offering a model for resistance surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While WGS is standard for bacterial outbreaks, its systematic application to track transmission and resistance evolution in Candida within a defined patient cohort is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive strain typing to a dynamic, population-genetic analysis of a fungal pathogen in a high-risk, real-world clinical environment.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Randomized Trial Evaluating the Impact of Same-Day, PCR-Based Candida Species and Azole Resistance Detection on Clinical Outcomes in Women with Complicated Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent and azole-resistant vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) present major therapeutic challenges, often requiring multiple empiric treatment attempts before effective therapy is identified. Current culture-based diagnostics are slow, delaying targeted treatment. Rapid molecular panels for bloodstream infections have revolutionized sepsis management by providing same-day pathogen and resistance identification, but this approach has not been systematically applied to VVC.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a rapid, multiplex PCR panel capable of detecting common Candida species and key azole resistance markers (e.g., ERG11 mutations) from vaginal swabs will lead to faster initiation of effective antifungal therapy and improve clinical and mycological cure rates at the test-of-cure visit compared to standard culture and susceptibility testing.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized controlled trial. Women presenting with suspected complicated VVC will be randomized 1:1 to an intervention arm (vaginal swab tested on a modified version of the BioFire® FilmArray® Blood Culture Identification panel, adapted to include Candida species and resistance markers, with results within 4 hours) or a standard-of-care arm (routine culture and susceptibility testing). The primary endpoint will be clinical and mycological cure at day 14. Secondary endpoints include time to effective therapy, symptom resolution, and patient satisfaction. Statistical analysis will use intention-to-treat principles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the clinical utility of rapid molecular diagnostics in VVC. It will establish whether a 'sepsis-style' diagnostic paradigm can improve outcomes in a common gynecologic infection and generate data on the prevalence of azole resistance markers in a clinical cohort. Findings could directly inform new diagnostic and treatment algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While rapid panels are standard in sepsis, their adaptation and rigorous clinical evaluation for VVC is novel. This proposal moves beyond observational studies by using an RCT design to measure the causal impact of rapid diagnostics on patient outcomes, bridging a critical gap between infectious disease diagnostics and gynecologic therapeutics.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of a Vaginal Lactobacillus crispatus Probiotic for the Prevention of Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis Episodes
proposals.motivation: Despite effective antifungals like fluconazole and ibrexafungerp, recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) remains a prevalent and debilitating condition, indicating a failure of purely pathogen-focused strategies. Pre-2026 research increasingly recognizes that host factors, particularly a resilient vaginal microbiome dominated by Lactobacillus species, are critical for maintaining mucosal homeostasis and preventing Candida overgrowth. Shifting the therapeutic paradigm from antifungal suppression to sustained host-microbiome restoration addresses a fundamental limitation of current care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained vaginal colonization with Lactobacillus crispatus, achieved through periodic probiotic administration, will reduce the frequency of symptomatic RVVC episodes by enhancing local mucosal immunity and creating a microenvironment resistant to Candida proliferation, independent of concurrent antifungal use.
proposals.method: We will conduct a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial enrolling 180 women with a history of RVVC. Participants will be randomized 1:1 to receive either a vaginal suppository containing a defined strain of L. crispatus (CTV-05) or an identical placebo, administered twice weekly for 12 months, with no protocol-mandated antifungals. The primary endpoint is the number of culture-confirmed symptomatic VVC episodes during the intervention period. Secondary endpoints include time to first recurrence, vaginal microbiome composition (by 16S rRNA gene sequencing), mucosal immune markers (e.g., beta-defensins, IL-22), and patient-reported outcomes. Analysis will be by intention-to-treat.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide Level I evidence for a microbiome-modulating, non-antifungal strategy for RVVC prevention. It will delineate the clinical efficacy of a specific probiotic regimen and correlate outcomes with mechanistic host factors, moving the field toward personalized, ecologically based management. The findings could establish a new first-line preventative approach, reducing reliance on chronic antifungal exposure.
proposals.novelty: While probiotics have been studied as adjuncts, this trial uniquely positions a defined host-microbiome modulator as the sole, primary preventative intervention against RVVC, explicitly testing the "host-first" paradigm. It prospectively links clinical recurrence to detailed longitudinal microbiome and host immune profiling, beyond the correlative studies available pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial of an Antifungal Stewardship Intervention to Optimize Prescribing and Reduce Azole Resistance in Outpatient Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: The rise of azole-resistant Candida species, particularly Candida glabrata, in vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is a growing public health concern, driven in part by empiric and inappropriate antifungal use. Antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) are standard in inpatient settings for antibiotics but have not been systematically adapted for outpatient antifungal use, especially in gynecology clinics where most VVC is managed. Implementing a structured stewardship model is critical to preserve the efficacy of existing agents like fluconazole and newer drugs like ibrexafungerp.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a multi-faceted antifungal stewardship program in outpatient gynecology clinics will significantly reduce inappropriate fluconazole prescriptions for VVC and, over time, decrease the prevalence of azole-resistant Candida isolates compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized, controlled trial across 20-30 outpatient gynecology practices. Intervention clinics will implement a stewardship bundle including: 1) a validated clinical decision support algorithm integrated into the EMR to guide appropriate testing (culture, susceptibility) and agent selection based on symptoms and risk factors; 2) provider education on IDSA VVC guidelines and resistance patterns; and 3) audit and feedback on prescribing metrics. Control clinics will provide usual care. Primary outcomes will be the rate of guideline-concordant prescribing and the proportion of Candida cultures with azole resistance at 12-month follow-up.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the feasibility and impact of antifungal stewardship in an outpatient gynecological setting. It will yield a practical, transferable intervention model to optimize antifungal use. The results will directly inform clinical guidelines on managing VVC in an era of increasing resistance.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sobel's prior work has focused on testing new antifungal agents (e.g., ibrexafungerp) and partner treatment, this study innovatively applies the established framework of antimicrobial stewardship—previously confined largely to inpatient antibiotics—to the distinct challenge of outpatient antifungal resistance, creating a proactive, systems-based approach to preserve therapeutic efficacy.

proposals.title: Development and Preclinical Evaluation of a Sustained-Release Intravaginal Ring for Long-Acting Prophylaxis of Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) requires frequent, often weekly or monthly, oral or topical antifungal dosing, which leads to poor adherence and suboptimal outcomes. Existing therapies, including the recently approved oral ibrexafungerp, are systemic or short-acting, and there is a lack of localized, long-acting delivery options that could improve efficacy and convenience for patients, particularly in the context of emerging azole resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting sustained-release drug delivery platforms, specifically intravaginal ring (IVR) technology, for the localized delivery of a novel antifungal (e.g., ibrexafungerp) will provide prolonged, therapeutic drug levels in vaginal tissue, effectively preventing Candida recurrence in a preclinical model.
proposals.method: We will formulate IVRs using medical-grade silicone elastomer or thermoplastic polyurethane, loaded with ibrexafungerp. In vitro release kinetics will be characterized using USP apparatus 4. We will employ a established murine model of recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis to test efficacy. Rings will be inserted following primary infection clearance, and fungal burden, host immune markers, and drug concentrations in vaginal tissue will be quantified over a 28-day follow-up period, comparing the IVR to placebo rings and conventional oral fluconazole dosing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide proof-of-concept for a novel, patient-centric delivery system for RVVC prophylaxis, potentially extending the dosing interval to months. It will generate critical preclinical pharmacokinetic and efficacy data for ibrexafungerp in a sustained-release format, informing future clinical trial design for long-acting antifungal therapies.
proposals.novelty: While IVRs are established for contraception and HIV prevention, their application for delivering novel antifungals like ibrexafungerp for RVVC prophylaxis is unexplored. This approach directly translates oncology/dermatology principles of sustained, localized delivery to a gynecologic infection with high recurrence rates.

proposals.title: Host and Microbial Factors Associated with Sustained Fluconazole Susceptibility in Women with Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis on Long-term Suppressive Therapy: A Case-Control Study
proposals.motivation: The emergence of azole-resistant Candida species, particularly Candida glabrata and C. auris, is a growing clinical challenge in the management of recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC). While prior research focuses on mechanisms of resistance, understanding why some women on chronic fluconazole suppression remain infection-free with susceptible isolates could reveal novel protective factors. Identifying these factors is critical for developing strategies to prevent resistance and preserve the utility of first-line azole therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Women with RVVC who maintain long-term clinical control with fluconazole suppression without developing microbiological resistance possess distinct host immunological, genetic, or vaginal microbiome profiles, and/or are colonized by Candida strains with intrinsic genetic stability against azole resistance development.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective case-control study nested within existing clinical trial cohorts of women on ≥12 months of fluconazole suppression for RVVC. Cases (controllers) will be women with sustained clinical resolution and fluconazole-susceptible Candida isolates at all follow-ups. Controls (non-controllers) will be matched women who developed microbiologically confirmed fluconazole-resistant recurrence. We will analyze stored vaginal swabs using: 1) whole-genome sequencing of serial Candida isolates to identify genetic signatures of stability, 2) 16S rRNA and ITS2 sequencing to characterize the bacterial and fungal microbiota, and 3) Luminex-based assays on vaginal lavage fluid to profile host immune mediators (e.g., cytokines, defensins). Statistical analyses will include multivariate logistic regression to identify independent protective factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will shift the paradigm from reactive study of resistance to proactive identification of factors that prevent it. It will generate the first comprehensive profile of the host-microbe interface associated with sustained azole susceptibility, offering potential biomarkers for patient stratification. Findings could inform the development of adjunctive therapies (e.g., probiotics, immunomodulators) aimed at extending the effective lifespan of fluconazole.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the standard resistance research framework by focusing on the understudied 'protected' phenotype. It integrates longitudinal Candida genomics with concurrent host immune and microbiome profiling, a multi-omics approach not previously applied to this specific clinical question in RVVC.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Symptomatic Response in Candida glabrata Vaginitis: A Clinical and Molecular Study of Inflammatory Cytokines and Neuronal Sensitization Markers
proposals.motivation: Candida glabrata is an increasingly common cause of vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC), often presenting with symptoms despite minimal inflammation, unlike the neutrophil-dominated response in C. albicans VVC. This clinical observation challenges the paradigm that symptoms are directly inflammation-mediated. Understanding the pathophysiology of C. glabrata vaginitis is critical for developing targeted symptomatic therapies, especially given its intrinsic azole resistance and the limited efficacy of current antifungals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that symptoms in C. glabrata vaginitis are primarily driven by non-inflammatory pathways, specifically direct fungal-mediated sensitization of local vaginal afferent nerves, rather than by a classic inflammatory cytokine response.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 120 women with culture-confirmed C. glabrata vaginitis, 40 with C. albicans VVC, and 40 healthy controls. Symptom severity will be quantified using a standardized visual analog scale (VAS). Vaginal lavage samples will be analyzed via multiplex ELISA for a panel of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and neuronal sensitization markers (NGF, BDNF, Substance P). Correlations between symptom scores and biomarker levels will be analyzed using Spearman's rank correlation and multivariate regression, controlling for fungal burden.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive molecular profile of the symptomatic C. glabrata vaginal environment, directly testing the inflammation-symptom link. It will establish whether neuronal sensitization pathways are a dominant mechanism, offering a novel therapeutic target (e.g., neuro-modulatory agents) for a condition with poor treatment responses. The findings will refine the pathophysiological model of VVC.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized the robust inflammatory response in C. albicans VVC, the symptomatic mechanism in the minimally inflammatory C. glabrata infection remains unexplored. This proposal shifts focus from immune recruitment to direct neuro-epithelial interactions as a cause of symptoms, a non-obvious pathway given existing inflammatory models of VVC.

proposals.title: Impact of Bacterial Vaginosis-Associated Microbiota on Host Immune Responses to Candida glabrata: A Clinical and Ex Vivo Study
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) caused by non-albicans species like Candida glabrata is a growing therapeutic challenge, often associated with azole resistance. Clinical observations suggest that co-occurrence with bacterial vaginosis (BV) is common and may alter disease presentation and treatment response. However, pre-2026 research on polymicrobial interactions in vulvovaginal candidiasis has focused almost exclusively on C. albicans, leaving a critical gap in understanding how BV-associated bacteria modulate the host immune response to C. glabrata.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-infection with Gardnerella vaginalis and other BV-associated bacteria will significantly alter the vaginal epithelial immune response to C. glabrata, leading to a distinct cytokine profile compared to C. glabrata monoinfection, and that this altered response correlates with clinical symptoms and mycological outcomes in women with co-infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of women with symptomatic vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) caused by C. glabrata, with and without concurrent BV diagnosed by Nugent score. Vaginal lavage samples will be collected at diagnosis. Ex vivo, a 3D human vaginal epithelial cell model will be infected with C. glabrata alone or in combination with G. vaginalis. Host response will be quantified via multiplex cytokine/chemokine analysis (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, IL-17A, IL-23, S100A8) of clinical samples and culture supernatants. Clinical follow-up will assess symptom scores and mycological cure after standard antifungal therapy (e.g., ibrexafungerp or fluconazole, based on susceptibility). Statistical analysis will compare immune profiles and outcomes between monoinfected and co-infected groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical and mechanistic data on how a prevalent co-infection (BV) shapes the host environment for C. glabrata. It will identify specific immune signatures associated with polymicrobial VVC, offering potential biomarkers for disease stratification. The findings could explain differential treatment outcomes and inform the development of more effective, tailored therapeutic strategies for RVVC caused by non-albicans Candida.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied C. albicans-BV interactions, this proposal uniquely shifts focus to the increasingly problematic C. glabrata. It moves beyond descriptive epidemiology to directly test the immune-modulating effect of specific BV bacteria on the host response to this less virulent but persistent pathogen.

proposals.title: In Vivo Evaluation of C. glabrata Adhesin and Biofilm Genes as Virulence Determinants in a Murine Model of Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Candida glabrata is a leading cause of recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) and is inherently less susceptible to azole antifungals, complicating treatment. While in vitro studies have identified potential virulence factors like adhesins (Epa family) and biofilm formation genes, their specific contribution to in vivo colonization and persistence during infection remains poorly characterized. Establishing a direct causal link between these factors and disease is critical for identifying novel therapeutic targets for this difficult-to-treat pathogen.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific C. glabrata virulence genes, particularly those encoding epithelial adhesins (EPA genes) and regulators of biofilm formation, are essential for establishing and maintaining vaginal colonization in vivo. Mutant strains lacking these genes will demonstrate significantly reduced fungal burden and persistence in a murine model of vulvovaginal candidiasis compared to the wild-type parental strain.
proposals.method: We will utilize a well-established estrogen-dependent murine model of vulvovaginal candidiasis. Isogenic knockout mutants of C. glabrata (e.g., Δepa1, Δepa6, Δbcr1) will be generated via homologous recombination or CRISPR-Cas9 techniques. Groups of mice (n=10-12 per strain) will be inoculated with either wild-type or mutant strains. Vaginal fungal burden will be quantified by serial plating of lavage fluid on selective agar at days 1, 4, 7, and 14 post-inoculation. Tissue adherence and inflammatory response will be assessed histologically. Statistical analysis will compare colony-forming unit (CFU) counts over time between groups using repeated-measures ANOVA.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct in vivo validation of key C. glabrata virulence mechanisms, moving beyond correlative in vitro data. It will identify which specific factors are non-redundant for infection in a physiologically relevant host environment. The findings will establish a prioritized list of molecular targets for future development of anti-virulence or adjunctive therapies aimed at preventing RVVC caused by C. glabrata.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sobel's group and others has focused on clinical trials and treatment outcomes, this proposal applies a targeted molecular genetics approach within a proven in vivo model specifically for C. glabrata VVC. It directly tests the functional necessity of candidate genes in disease pathogenesis, a step not yet taken in the clinical mycology literature as of 2025.

proposals.title: Transcriptomic Profiling of Vaginal Epithelium During Asymptomatic Candida glabrata Colonization: Defining the Host Tolerant State
proposals.motivation: Candida glabrata is an increasingly prevalent cause of vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC), notable for its reduced azole susceptibility. While symptomatic infection is well-studied, the mechanisms enabling asymptomatic colonization—a potential reservoir for recurrence—are poorly understood. Defining the host epithelial response during this commensal-like state is critical to understanding the transition to symptomatic disease and identifying novel targets for interventions that prevent recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that asymptomatic C. glabrata colonization is characterized by a distinct, tolerogenic transcriptional signature in vaginal epithelial cells, involving dampened inflammatory pathways and enhanced epithelial barrier integrity genes, compared to the inflammatory signature of symptomatic infection.
proposals.method: We will perform bulk RNA-seq on vaginal epithelial cells collected via swab/brush from three well-phenotyped participant groups: women with asymptomatic C. glabrata colonization (n=20), women with acute symptomatic C. glabrata VVC (n=20), and Candida-negative healthy controls (n=20). Differential gene expression analysis will be conducted using DESeq2, with gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) to identify modulated pathways. Key findings will be validated via qRT-PCR on an independent cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive map of the host epithelial transcriptome during asymptomatic C. glabrata carriage. It will identify specific host pathways associated with the 'tolerant' state, offering potential biomarkers for colonization risk and novel host-directed therapeutic targets to prevent the transition to symptomatic infection, complementing existing antifungal drug development.
proposals.novelty: While transcriptomic studies exist for symptomatic C. albicans VVC, this is the first to apply RNA-seq specifically to the clinically important but understudied state of asymptomatic C. glabrata colonization, focusing on the host epithelial compartment rather than the fungal pathogen or general immune response.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Composite Vaginitis Burden Score (VBS) for Health Policy and Resource Allocation
proposals.motivation: Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) and bacterial vaginosis (BV) are highly prevalent, chronic, and recurrent conditions causing significant morbidity, yet their systemic health and economic impact is often minimized in policy discussions. Current assessments are fragmented, lacking a standardized metric to compare its burden to other chronic diseases like asthma or migraine, which receive greater research and clinical priority. A rigorous, composite burden score is urgently needed to advocate for increased funding and guideline development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-domain Vaginitis Burden Score (VBS), integrating epidemiological, economic, and quality-of-life data, will quantify a disease burden comparable to or exceeding other chronic conditions with established policy priority, providing an objective tool for health system stakeholders.
proposals.method: We will construct the VBS using a multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) framework, weighting four pre-defined domains: 1) Prevalence/Incidence (from NHANES and clinical trial data), 2) Direct Costs (medication, outpatient visits), 3) Indirect Costs (lost productivity via Work Productivity and Activity Impairment questionnaire), and 4) QALY Decrements (using EQ-5D and condition-specific VAS). Domain weights will be established via a Delphi panel of 15-20 experts (clinicians, health economists). The final VBS will be calculated as a weighted sum and benchmarked against published burden metrics for migraine and asthma.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, composite metric to quantify the total population-level burden of vaginitis. It will deliver a policy-ready tool for health economists and advocacy groups to demonstrate the condition's significance, directly informing research funding appeals and clinical guideline development by bodies like the CDC and IDSA.
proposals.novelty: While burden studies exist, none have synthesized the multi-dimensional impact of vaginitis into a single, benchmarked score using an expert-weighted MCDA framework. This moves beyond qualitative description or single-domain cost analyses to create a directly comparable policy instrument.

proposals.title: Beyond the Nuisance: A Prospective Cohort Study Quantifying the Multidimensional Burden and Systemic Inflammatory Profile of Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) is a common condition affecting millions of women, yet it is often clinically dismissed as a trivial, localized infection. This perception, analogous to historical views of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), fails to account for its potential as a chronic inflammatory state with significant quality-of-life, economic, and long-term health impacts. A systematic, multidimensional burden assessment is needed to shift the clinical paradigm and justify more serious research and therapeutic investment.
proposals.hypothesis: Women with RVVC experience a quantifiable, multidimensional burden (symptomatic, psychological, economic, and sexual) that correlates with measurable local and systemic inflammatory markers, and this burden is comparable in impact to other recognized chronic inflammatory conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month prospective cohort study enrolling 200 women with RVVC (≥4 culture-confirmed episodes/year) and 100 matched controls. Burden will be quantified using validated instruments for pain (VAS), depression/anxiety (PHQ-9, GAD-7), sexual function (FSFI), and direct/indirect costs. Inflammatory profiling will involve serial cervicovaginal lavage and serum sampling to measure cytokines (e.g., IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, IL-17A) via multiplex ELISA and assess epithelial barrier integrity via quantification of tight junction proteins. Data will be analyzed using multivariate regression and structural equation modeling to map relationships between clinical severity, burden domains, and inflammatory markers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, longitudinal dataset linking the clinical presentation of RVVC to quantifiable burden and objective inflammatory dysregulation. It will establish a new evidence-based framework for classifying RVVC as a chronic inflammatory condition, directly informing future clinical guidelines, health policy, and the development of targeted anti-inflammatory adjuncts to antifungal therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sobel and others has focused on antifungal efficacy, this proposal innovatively applies a chronic disease model from gastroenterology (IBD) to vaginitis, systematically investigating extra-local burden and systemic inflammation—a perspective largely absent from pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Trial of a Rapid Molecular Diagnostic Panel Versus Standard Clinical Diagnosis for the Management of Acute Symptomatic Vaginitis: Impact on Treatment Success, Recurrence, and Patient-Reported Outcomes.
proposals.motivation: Vaginitis remains a leading reason for gynecologic visits, yet current empiric management, based on microscopy and clinical signs, is notoriously imprecise, with misdiagnosis rates exceeding 50% for mixed or non-albicans infections. This diagnostic inaccuracy drives a cycle of treatment failure, recurrence, antibiotic/antifungal misuse, and profound patient suffering, which is often dismissed as a 'minor' quality-of-life issue. The development of rapid, multiplex PCR panels for vaginitis pathogens (e.g., Candida spp., bacterial vaginosis-associated bacteria, Trichomonas) offers a potential paradigm shift but requires rigorous evaluation of its clinical and humanistic impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a rapid, point-of-care molecular diagnostic panel for symptomatic vaginitis will lead to superior clinical cure rates at follow-up and significantly reduce patient-reported measures of physical and psychosocial suffering compared to management guided by standard clinical diagnosis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, single-blind trial. 600 participants with acute symptomatic vaginitis will be randomized (1:1) to either the intervention arm (diagnosis via a rapid, FDA-cleared multiplex PCR panel performed at enrollment) or the control arm (diagnosis via standard microscopy, Amsel criteria, and KOH wet mount). All participants will receive syndrome-specific therapy per CDC guidelines, but treatment in the intervention arm will be precisely targeted to the PCR result. Primary outcomes are clinical cure at the Test-of-Cure visit (Day 10-14) and patient-reported burden measured by a validated Vaginitis-Specific Quality of Life scale at Day 7 and Day 28. Secondary outcomes include microbiologic cure, time to symptom resolution, and need for additional therapy within 60 days.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from a randomized trial on whether a precision diagnostic strategy improves objective clinical outcomes and reduces the multidimensional burden of vaginitis. It will generate critical data on the cost-benefit analysis of molecular testing in routine care and establish a patient-centered framework for evaluating diagnostic interventions in women's health.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sobel's prior work has focused on testing new therapeutics (e.g., ibrexafungerp) in defined populations, this proposal innovatively tests a diagnostic strategy as the primary therapeutic intervention. It moves beyond proving test accuracy to directly linking diagnostic precision to tangible reductions in human suffering, a novel endpoint in vaginitis research.

proposals.title: Prevalence and Clinical Significance of Non-Albicans and Azole-Resistant Candida Species in Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Comprehensive Microbiological and Clinical Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Current clinical paradigms for recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) predominantly focus on C. albicans, with treatment guidelines centered on fluconazole and newer agents like ibrexafungerp. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence indicates a rising prevalence of non-albicans Candida species and azole-resistant strains, which may be underdiagnosed and contribute to treatment failure. A systematic investigation is needed to define the true etiological spectrum of RVVC beyond the common causes to optimize therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant proportion of women with clinically diagnosed RVVC, particularly those with suboptimal response to standard azole therapy, are infected with non-albicans Candida species or azole-resistant C. albicans strains that are not identified by conventional diagnostic approaches.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study enrolling 300 women with RVVC. Vaginal samples will undergo comprehensive microbiological analysis, including CHROMagar culture for species identification and broth microdilution antifungal susceptibility testing (CLSI M27/EUCAST) against fluconazole, itraconazole, and ibrexafungerp. Next-generation sequencing (ITS2 region) will be performed on culture-negative or discordant samples to detect rare or fastidious fungi. Clinical data, including prior treatment history and symptom scores, will be correlated with microbiological findings over a 12-month follow-up period.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a detailed, contemporary profile of the mycological causes of RVVC, quantifying the burden of non-albicans and resistant species. It will directly inform the need for refined diagnostic algorithms and the potential role of culture and susceptibility testing in managing refractory cases. The data will also offer a baseline for evaluating the spectrum of activity of newer antifungal agents like ibrexafungerp against diverse pathogens.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies note the existence of alternative Candida species, this proposal systematically integrates advanced mycological techniques (susceptibility testing, sequencing) with longitudinal clinical follow-up in a large RVVC cohort to challenge the assumption that three main causes cover all cases, explicitly linking rare etiologies to treatment outcomes.

proposals.title: A Mandatory Translational Loop: Protocol for a Prospective, Parallel-Cohort Study Integrating Deep Phenotyping with the Ibrexafungerp Phase 3 Trial for Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of clinical trials for vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) and bacterial vaginosis (BV), fundamental gaps persist in understanding disease pathogenesis, recurrence drivers, and patient-specific treatment responses. The current model of drug development, focused narrowly on regulatory endpoints, fails to generate the mechanistic data needed to advance the field beyond empiric therapy and address rising azole resistance. This disconnect hinders the development of targeted, durable treatments for recurrent infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Mandating a parallel, deeply phenotyped research cohort within a pivotal antifungal trial will identify distinct molecular and microbiological signatures that correlate with clinical outcomes, thereby elucidating mechanisms of drug efficacy and failure while refining pathogenic etiologies of VVC.
proposals.method: This proposal designs a mandatory sub-study protocol to be embedded within a Phase 3, placebo-controlled trial of ibrexafungerp for recurrent VVC. All participants will be enrolled in the primary efficacy trial. A consecutively enrolled subset (n=200) will comprise the parallel translational cohort, undergoing intensive sampling at baseline, during acute episodes, and at follow-up. Samples will undergo: 1) deep sequencing (ITS2/LSU for fungi, 16S V4 for bacteria), 2) multiplex cytokine/chemokine profiling of vaginal fluid, and 3) targeted metabolomics. Data will be integrated using multivariate analysis (e.g., sparse Partial Least Squares Discriminant Analysis) and unsupervised clustering to define patient endotypes linked to ibrexafungerp response, recurrence timing, and mucosal immune patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, implementable blueprint for coupling drug development with etiology research. It will generate the first high-dimensional dataset directly linking a novel antifungal's clinical performance to the host-microbe interface in RVVC, moving beyond Candida albicans detection to functional pathogenesis. The model aims to create a sustainable feedback loop where therapeutic advances directly refine disease classification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty is structural and enforceable, proposing a mandatory, funded translational component as a non-negotiable element of vaginitis drug trials, rather than an optional add-on. This shifts the paradigm from viewing pathogenesis research as separate from drug development to an integrated, iterative process.

proposals.title: Beyond Antifungals: A Prospective Cohort Study to Define the Host-Microbe-Environmental Determinants of Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis (RVVC) Pathogenesis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) imposes a severe burden, affecting ~138 million women annually with significant impacts on quality of life, mental health, and economic productivity. Despite new antifungal agents like ibrexafungerp, treatment remains suppressive, not curative, due to a fundamental gap in understanding the complex, multifactorial etiology of recurrence. This perpetuates a cycle of symptomatic relief without addressing root causes, representing a critical unmet need in women's health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RVVC is driven by a stable, dysregulated host-vaginal ecosystem characterized by specific, measurable defects in local immune surveillance, epithelial barrier integrity, and microbiome resilience, which are not corrected by antifungal therapy alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 24-month longitudinal, observational cohort study of 300 women with RVVC and 100 matched controls. Using pre-2026 technologies, we will perform deep phenotyping at baseline and during symptomatic/remission phases. This includes longitudinal vaginal sampling for metagenomic sequencing (shotgun) and fungal/bacterial culturing, multiplex cytokine/antimicrobial peptide profiling (Luminex), and epithelial transcriptomics (RNA-seq) from cytobrush samples. Clinical and behavioral data will be captured via digital diaries. Multi-omics data integration will use multivariate statistical modeling (generalized linear mixed models) and network analysis to identify predictive signatures of recurrence.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from a purely drug-centric model to a mechanistic framework by defining the host and microbial signatures predictive of RVVC. It will identify novel, druggable targets for disease interception and provide the foundational data needed to develop personalized, preventive strategies, ultimately aiming to break the cycle of recurrence.
proposals.novelty: While prior RVVC studies focus on antifungal efficacy or isolated factors, this proposal integrates longitudinal multi-omics and clinical data to model the dynamic, systemic pathogenesis of recurrence, explicitly shifting the research paradigm from treatment to mechanistic understanding and prevention.

proposals.title: Metagenomic Pathogen Discovery in Women with Idiopathic Vulvovaginal Symptoms: A Case-Control Study
proposals.motivation: A significant proportion of women with vulvovaginal symptoms, such as burning, irritation, and discharge, do not receive a definitive diagnosis despite standard testing for Candida, bacterial vaginosis, and trichomoniasis. This idiopathic vaginitis leads to patient frustration, inappropriate antimicrobial use, and a lack of targeted therapeutic trials. The successful application of metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) for pathogen discovery in encephalitis and pneumonia provides a powerful model to address this analogous diagnostic gap in gynecology.
proposals.hypothesis: Deep metagenomic sequencing of vaginal samples from women with idiopathic symptoms, followed by computational subtraction of known host and microbiome sequences, will reveal novel, fastidious, or unculturable microbial agents not detected by conventional diagnostics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a case-control study comparing vaginal lavage samples from 50 women with idiopathic symptoms (cases) to 50 asymptomatic, matched controls. Total nucleic acid will be extracted and subjected to deep shotgun sequencing on an Illumina platform. Bioinformatic analysis will use the SURPI (Sequence-based Ultra-Rapid Pathogen Identification) pipeline for rapid classification. A rigorous subtraction protocol will sequentially remove human reads (via alignment to GRCh38) and known vaginal microbiota reads (via alignment to curated databases like RefSeq and the Human Microbiome Project). The remaining unassigned reads will be assembled de novo using metaSPAdes, and contigs will be annotated for potential coding sequences and compared against viral and microbial protein databases.
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first comprehensive, culture-independent catalog of novel microbial signatures associated with idiopathic vaginitis. It will provide preliminary evidence for new candidate pathogens, enabling future development of targeted molecular diagnostics and guiding hypothesis-driven research into their pathogenic mechanisms. The findings could redefine the etiologic landscape of vaginitis and inform the design of future antimicrobial trials.
proposals.novelty: While mNGS has been used to describe the vaginal microbiome composition, its systematic application as a pathogen discovery tool for idiopathic clinical syndromes in gynecology, using a rigorous case-control design and a subtraction-based bioinformatics pipeline adapted from systemic infection studies, is novel and represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Co-Designed, Point-of-Care Diagnostic System for the Etiological Diagnosis of Vaginitis: A Mixed-Methods Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: Current vaginitis management is predominantly empiric, leading to misdiagnosis, inappropriate antimicrobial use, and patient dissatisfaction, particularly for recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) and mixed infections. Pre-2026, while new treatments like ibrexafungerp are emerging, a definitive shift from empiric therapy is hindered by the lack of rapid, accurate, and implementable diagnostic tools accessible in diverse clinical settings. This gap perpetuates suboptimal care and complicates the targeted use of novel antifungals.
proposals.hypothesis: A diagnostic system for vaginitis (candidiasis, bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis) co-designed with end-users (clinicians, lab staff, patients) will demonstrate superior technical performance (sensitivity/specificity >95% vs. PCR) and higher rates of successful implementation and clinician adoption in routine practice compared to standard-of-care microscopy and clinical algorithms.
proposals.method: We will employ a parallel, mixed-methods design. Phase 1 (Co-Design): We will conduct iterative focus groups and prototyping sessions with OB/GYNs, primary care providers, microbiologists, and patients to define requirements for a point-of-care nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) system, prioritizing speed (<30 min), simplicity, and cost. Phase 2 (Technical Validation): A multicenter diagnostic accuracy study will enroll 500 symptomatic women to compare the co-designed prototype against a composite reference standard (culture, PCR, Gram stain) using pre-specified analytical algorithms for multiplex detection and semi-quantification. Phase 3 (Implementation): A pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in 20 diverse clinics will assess adoption using the tailored implementation toolkit (guidelines, cost-analysis, workflow maps) versus usual dissemination.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a validated, implementation-ready diagnostic system that directly addresses the clinical need for precise etiological diagnosis, enabling targeted therapy. It will provide a novel model for diagnostic development that integrates human factors engineering and implementation science from the outset, generating a blueprint for moving other syndromic conditions beyond empiric management.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fully integrated, parallel focus on technical validation and real-world implementation via co-design, a approach not yet applied to vaginitis diagnostics. It moves beyond simply evaluating a pre-existing test to actively designing one with embedded implementation strategies, ensuring the output is both accurate and readily adoptable.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Point-of-Care Triage Algorithm for Empiric Therapy in Women with Vulvovaginitis
proposals.motivation: Current management of vaginitis often relies on empiric therapy, which can fail in cases of mixed infections or azole-resistant Candida, leading to poor patient outcomes and repeat visits. Conversely, comprehensive lab testing for all patients is costly and delays treatment. A rapid, point-of-care triage tool is needed to guide initial therapy decisions, aligning with growing emphasis on antimicrobial stewardship and personalized care in women's health.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinical prediction algorithm, incorporating rapid point-of-care test results for pH, amines, and beta-glucan, can accurately identify women with uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) who will respond to empiric fluconazole, distinguishing them from those with complex presentations requiring further diagnostic workup.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multicenter diagnostic study enrolling women presenting with vaginitis symptoms. At the point-of-care, participants will undergo a standardized panel: vaginal pH, KOH whiff test, and a lateral flow assay for Candida albicans (if available) or a rapid beta-glucan test. Clinical data (symptom score, prior antifungal use) will be collected. All participants will also receive gold-standard lab testing (culture, PCR for Candida species and bacterial vaginosis organisms). A multivariate logistic regression model will be developed to predict 'empiric therapy success' (defined as clinical cure with fluconazole/placebo in a linked RCT arm) versus 'need for advanced diagnostics'.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, actionable clinical algorithm to optimize the first visit for vaginitis, reducing inappropriate empiric therapy and its associated morbidity. It will provide a framework for integrating novel rapid diagnostics into a stewardship-focused clinical pathway, directly addressing the challenge of azole-resistant Candida highlighted in recent literature.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond simply improving diagnostic accuracy to creating a pragmatic triage tool that strategically allocates resources, an approach not yet validated in vaginitis care. It integrates emerging rapid biomarkers (e.g., beta-glucan) with classic clinical signs to specifically address the treatment-resistance problem.

proposals.title: Correlating Vaginal Mycobiome and Microbiome Composition with Patient-Reported Morbidity: A Molecular Epidemiology Study to Define High-Burden Pathogens in Recurrent Vaginitis
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in identifying Candida species and bacterial communities, a significant portion of vaginitis cases remain idiopathic or poorly managed, with patient suffering often minimized. The profound impact on quality of life—chronic pain, sexual dysfunction, and psychological distress—is well-documented but not systematically linked to specific microbial etiologies beyond standard pathogens. Pre-2026, next-generation sequencing allows for deep microbial profiling, yet these data are rarely integrated with granular, longitudinal patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) to quantify the true burden caused by known, emerging, or uncultivated microbes.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, quantifiable components of the vaginal mycobiome and microbiome, including non-albicans Candida, azole-resistant strains, and under-characterized bacteria or fungi, are directly associated with the severity and persistence of patient-reported physical and psychosocial morbidity in recurrent vaginitis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 300 women with recurrent vulvovaginal symptoms. Vaginal samples will undergo deep shotgun metagenomic sequencing (Illumina platform) and targeted ITS2/16S rRNA gene sequencing for comprehensive microbial profiling. Data will be correlated with detailed, validated PROMs collected at enrollment and over 6 months of follow-up, measuring pain (VAS), sexual function (FSFI), mental health (PHQ-9, GAD-7), and quality of life (VR-QoL). Statistical analyses will employ multivariate linear and mixed-effects models, controlling for confounders, and microbial network analysis (e.g., SPIEC-EASI) will identify co-occurring pathogenic consortia linked to high morbidity.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond microbial cataloguing to establish an evidence-based hierarchy of 'high-burden' pathogens based on their direct contribution to human suffering. It will provide a novel dataset linking deep molecular phenotypes to multidimensional morbidity, creating a framework to prioritize diagnostic targets and guide therapeutic development, such as for ibrexafungerp or novel agents, towards the microbes causing the greatest patient impact.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Sobel and others focuses on treatment efficacy for known pathogens, this proposal uniquely applies molecular epidemiology to the unsolved problem of idiopathic/recurrent vaginitis, directly testing the causal link between the expanded microbial community and the comprehensive suffering historically dismissed as a 'nuisance' condition.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Validate an Ecological Network-Based Diagnostic Platform for Predicting Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis Treatment Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) remains a major therapeutic challenge, with high recurrence rates after antifungal therapy like fluconazole or ibrexafungerp. Current diagnostics, based on pathogen presence/absence, fail to capture the ecological dysbiosis underlying recurrence, leading to empiric treatment that does not address the destabilized microbial network. A mechanism-based diagnostic that quantifies ecological resilience could guide targeted restorative interventions and improve long-term outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an 'Ecological Health Index' (EHI), derived from network analysis of the vaginal microbiome, will predict clinical response to antifungal therapy in RVVC and identify patients who would benefit from adjunctive, ecology-restoring interventions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of samples from a completed Phase 3 trial of ibrexafungerp vs. placebo for RVVC.
