Pre- and post-treatment vaginal metagenomic samples will be analyzed using co-occurrence network inference (SparCC algorithm) and functional profiling (HUMAnN2). The EHI will integrate metrics of network stability (modularity, connectivity), keystone species (betweenness centrality), and metabolic pathway disruption. Machine learning (LASSO regression) will model the EHI's ability to predict 6-month recurrence, compared to standard Candida culture/qPCR.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first validated, ecology-based diagnostic metric for vaginal health, moving beyond compositional lists to functional community structure. It will provide a novel biomarker to stratify RVVC patients for precision therapy, such as selecting candidates for specific probiotic regimens post-antifungal treatment. This directly addresses the clinical focus of Dr. Sobel's work on improving long-term treatment outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While microbiome composition is well-studied, applying ecological network theory and stability metrics to develop a clinically actionable diagnostic index for vaginitis is novel. This approach functionally integrates community interactions and resilience, a significant advance over static taxonomic profiling.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Multiplexed Point-of-Care Cartridge for the Syndromic Diagnosis of Vaginitis: A Diagnostic Accuracy Study
proposals.motivation: Current point-of-care diagnostics for vaginitis are often single-analyte, leading to misdiagnosis and inappropriate treatment, particularly given the polymicrobial and overlapping nature of conditions like bacterial vaginosis (BV), vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC), and trichomoniasis. This diagnostic uncertainty complicates clinical trials and patient management, as seen in studies of antifungals like ibrexafungerp and fluconazole, where accurate patient stratification is critical. A rapid, comprehensive diagnostic tool is urgently needed to guide etiology-specific therapy at the point of care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating established sensing modalities—lateral flow detection of sialidase and proline iminopeptidase for BV, loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) for Candida species RNA, and a paper-based microfluidic sensor for pH and amines—into a single multiplexed cartridge will yield a diagnostic output that, when interpreted by a validated clinical algorithm, provides accurate, syndromic diagnosis comparable to composite reference standards (Amsel's criteria, Nugent score, culture, and NAAT).
proposals.method: We will engineer a single-use, disposable cartridge with separate chambers for: 1) a lateral flow strip detecting BV-associated enzymes, 2) a LAMP reaction zone with Candida albicans and glabrata-specific primers, and 3) a paper microfluidic network with colorimetric indicators for pH and trimethylamine. A smartphone-based reader will quantify signals. The diagnostic algorithm will be developed using regression tree analysis on data from an initial cohort (n=200) with confirmed diagnoses, integrating all sensor outputs to generate a final readout of BV, VVC, trichomoniasis (via a separate LAMP channel), or non-infectious etiology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, integrated point-of-care device that moves beyond pathogen detection to deliver a syndromic diagnosis, directly addressing the clinical need for rapid differential diagnosis. It will establish a new diagnostic standard for pragmatic clinical trials, enabling precise patient enrollment for studies of new agents like ibrexafungerp. The system aims to reduce empirical treatment and improve patient outcomes in both routine care and research settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in inventing new sensors, but in the strategic repurposing and integration of proven, low-complexity technologies (LAMP, lateral flow, paper microfluidics) from virology and bacteriology into a single, unified platform specifically designed for the polymicrobial context of vaginitis, coupled with a clinically validated interpretation algorithm.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive, Multiplexed Point-of-Care Diagnostic Analysis of Symptomatic Vaginitis Cases with Negative Standard Workups: A Discovery Study for Novel Etiologies
proposals.motivation: Despite high prevalence, a significant subset of women with vaginitis symptoms receive negative results for the classic triad (Candida, bacterial vaginosis, Trichomonas) via standard microscopy and culture. This idiopathic group represents a major clinical dilemma, leading to empirical, often ineffective treatments and patient dissatisfaction. The assumption that the etiology of common vaginitis is fully characterized may be incorrect, potentially overlooking novel pathogens, microbial consortia, or host inflammatory signatures.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying high-resolution, multiplexed point-of-care diagnostic platforms to symptomatic women with negative standard testing will reveal previously unrecognized microbial, protein, or metabolic biomarkers, identifying novel etiologies for a subset of common vaginitis.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 symptomatic participants with negative standard clinical testing. Vaginal swabs will be analyzed using a pre-2026, commercially available multiplex PCR panel (e.g., BioFire® FilmArray® Torch system with a customized vaginal panel) to detect a broad range of bacteria, fungi, and viruses. Concurrently, we will deploy a lateral flow antigen array platform, adapted from virology research, to profile key host inflammatory proteins (e.g., IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-8, MMP-8). Microbial abundance data and protein levels will be integrated using multivariate analysis (e.g., principal component analysis, cluster analysis) to identify distinct biomarker profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution molecular profile of 'idiopathic' vaginitis, potentially defining new, treatable clinical syndromes. It will generate specific biomarker targets for future diagnostic development and challenge the current diagnostic paradigm. The findings could directly inform the design of subsequent interventional trials targeting newly identified etiological agents.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the purpose of a multiplex diagnostic from confirmation to discovery in a common clinical scenario. The synergistic use of multiplex nucleic acid detection and host protein profiling for etiological discovery in vaginitis is novel and moves beyond current culture- and syndrome-based approaches.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Multiplex, Point-of-Care Molecular Diagnostic Device for the Etiologic Differentiation of Vaginitis Syndromes
proposals.motivation: Current diagnosis of vaginitis (vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC), bacterial vaginosis (BV), and trichomoniasis) often relies on clinical signs and insensitive point-of-care (POC) tests, leading to misdiagnosis and inappropriate treatment, particularly problematic with emerging azole-resistant Candida species. Rapid, accurate, syndromic POC diagnostics, common in virology (e.g., multiplex PCR for respiratory viruses), are critically lacking in gynecology, hindering targeted therapy and clinical trial enrollment.
proposals.hypothesis: That a novel, integrated POC device, adapting nucleic acid amplification and lateral flow detection technologies from virology, can accurately and rapidly (<30 minutes) differentiate between Candida species (including resistant genotypes), BV-associated bacteria, and Trichomonas vaginalis from a single vaginal swab in a clinical setting.
proposals.method: The project will adapt and integrate two pre-2026 POC platforms: 1) an isothermal nucleic acid amplification system (e.g., recombinase polymerase amplification, RPA) for sensitive pathogen detection, and 2) a multiplex lateral flow immunoassay strip for visual readout of amplified targets. A proprietary microfluidic cartridge will be designed to automate sample processing from a single swab. The device will be validated against standard laboratory PCR and culture using vaginal samples from 300 symptomatic women enrolled in a clinical study, with analysis of sensitivity, specificity, and usability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully integrated, rapid molecular POC test for the comprehensive etiologic diagnosis of vaginitis. It will enable immediate, pathogen-specific treatment decisions at the initial visit, improving patient outcomes and antimicrobial stewardship. Furthermore, it will create a precise tool for stratifying patients in clinical trials, such as those for new antifungal agents like ibrexafungerp.
proposals.novelty: While multiplex lab-based PCR panels for vaginitis exist (pre-2026), this proposal innovates by miniaturizing and automating this capability into a true, single-step, rapid POC format—a technological leap not yet achieved. It specifically integrates detection of genetic markers for antifungal resistance, moving beyond simple species identification.

proposals.title: A Cross-Sectional Study of Psychosocial Distress and Sexual Dysfunction in Women with Subclinical versus Symptomatic Vulvovaginal Candidiasis and Bacterial Vaginosis
proposals.motivation: Current clinical management of vaginal infections is predicated on symptom reporting, yet many women harbor Candida species or dysbiotic BV-associated bacteria without overt symptoms. The potential psychosocial and sexual health burden of these subclinical states is unknown, representing a significant gap in understanding the true impact of vaginal dysbiosis. Quantifying this burden could challenge the binary symptomatic/asymptomatic paradigm and inform more holistic treatment thresholds.
proposals.hypothesis: Women with laboratory-confirmed, subclinical vulvovaginal candidiasis or bacterial vaginosis will report significantly greater psychosocial distress and sexual dysfunction compared to healthy controls, and will exhibit a psychological profile intermediate between that of symptomatic patients and healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will recruit 300 participants into three groups: symptomatic (meeting clinical and lab criteria), subclinical (positive Nugent score ≥7 or Candida culture/PCR without symptoms), and healthy controls. All will complete validated psychometric scales: the Female Sexual Distress Scale-Revised (FSDS-R), the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS), and the SF-12 for general well-being. Statistical analysis will employ multivariate analysis of covariance (MANCOVA) to compare scale scores across groups, controlling for covariates like age and relationship status, followed by post-hoc pairwise comparisons with Bonferroni correction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative data on the quality-of-life impact of subclinical vaginal dysbiosis, moving beyond pathogen detection alone. It will establish whether a continuum of psychosocial suffering exists, directly linked to microbial presence. The findings could redefine clinical endpoints in therapeutic trials (like those for ibrexafungerp) to include psychological metrics and justify intervention studies for asymptomatic carriers.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sobel's prior work has focused on treatment efficacy for symptomatic disease, this proposal applies psychometric tools to the understudied subclinical population. It integrates microbiological diagnosis with validated psychological scales to test a novel 'continuum of impact' model, which is not addressed in current placebo-controlled treatment trials.

proposals.title: A dynamic vaginal mucosa-on-a-chip model integrating mechanical strain and a defined Lactobacillus consortium to study pathogen disruption and evaluate antifungal efficacy against Candida albicans biofilms.
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) and bacterial vaginosis (BV) remain prevalent and difficult to manage, partly due to inadequate in vitro models that lack key physiological features. Existing static cell cultures fail to recapitulate the mechanical forces of the vaginal tract and the protective role of the resident microbiome, limiting our understanding of infection pathogenesis and the preclinical evaluation of new antifungals like ibrexafungerp.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating cyclic mechanical strain and a stable commensal microbiome into a vaginal mucosa-chip will create a more clinically relevant microenvironment, revealing novel mechanisms of Candida albicans biofilm formation and pathogen disruption by BV-associated bacteria that are not observable in static models.
proposals.method: We will use a commercial two-channel organ-chip system lined with primary human vaginal epithelial cells and fibroblasts. The epithelial channel will be subjected to programmable cyclic mechanical strain (5-15% elongation) to mimic physiological distension. A defined consortium of vaginal Lactobacillus species (e.g., L. crispatus, L. jensenii) will be introduced and maintained via continuous low-flow perfusion of a custom medium. Pathogen challenge studies will involve introducing C. albicans (azole-sensitive and resistant strains) or BV-associated bacteria (Gardnerella vaginalis) into the luminal channel. Real-time analysis will include transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER), multiplex cytokine profiling, confocal microscopy, and RNA sequencing of harvested cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, physiologically relevant platform for studying vulvovaginal infections, directly linking mechanical cues and microbiome context to pathogen behavior. It will provide a transformative tool for the preclinical assessment of antifungal agents, including ibrexafungerp, against biofilms in a human-relevant microenvironment, potentially identifying new therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: While organ-chips exist, none integrate both cyclic mechanical strain specific to the vaginal tract and a stable, defined commensal bacterial co-culture to study gynecological infections, making this a uniquely holistic model for RVVC and BV research.

proposals.title: Development and Application of a 3D Vaginal Epithelial Tissue Model to Elucidate Pathogenesis Mechanisms and Drug Efficacy in Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) remains a debilitating condition for many women, with treatment options like fluconazole facing challenges from resistance and recurrence. Pre-2026, pathogenesis studies and drug screening rely heavily on animal models or simple 2D cell cultures, which poorly recapitulate the complex human vaginal mucosal environment and host-pathogen interactions. A physiologically relevant in vitro model is critically needed to dissect infection mechanisms and evaluate new antifungals like ibrexafungerp in a more human-relevant system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a three-dimensional (3D) tissue-engineered model of the vaginal epithelium will more accurately mimic key aspects of RVVC pathogenesis—including Candida albicans adhesion, invasion, and epithelial damage—than existing models, and will reveal differential efficacy of antifungal agents based on their mechanism of action.
proposals.method: We will construct a 3D stratified squamous epithelial model using immortalized human vaginal epithelial cells (VK2/E6E7) cultured at an air-liquid interface on collagen-coated transwell inserts to promote differentiation and cornification. This model will be characterized via histology (H&E, cytokeratin staining) and transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER). For pathogenesis studies, we will infect models with clinical isolates of C. albicans (including azole-resistant strains) and quantify fungal burden (CFU), tissue integrity (LDH release), and cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-8) over 24-72 hours. For drug testing, infected models will be treated with fluconazole, ibrexafungerp, or placebo, followed by assessment of fungal viability and epithelial damage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with a robust, reproducible, and human-relevant platform to study RVVC pathogenesis beyond planktonic culture. It will generate novel insights into host epithelial responses to infection and directly compare the tissue-level pharmacodynamics of established and novel antifungal agents. The model will serve as a foundational tool for future high-throughput drug screening and mechanistic studies of vaginal infections.
proposals.novelty: While 3D models exist for other mucosal sites, the application of a fully differentiated, air-liquid interface vaginal epithelium model specifically for RVVC pathogenesis and antifungal efficacy testing is novel. This approach moves beyond the participant-based clinical trial or simple in vitro susceptibility testing that dominates pre-2026 research in Dr. Sobel's portfolio.

proposals.title: A Global, Geographically-Stratified Diagnostic and Treatment Algorithm for Vaginitis: A Meta-Analytic Synthesis of Pathogen Prevalence and Resistance Patterns to Guide Precision Therapy
proposals.motivation: Current management of vaginitis (bacterial vaginosis, vulvovaginal candidiasis, trichomoniasis) relies heavily on empiric therapy, which fails in regions with high rates of azole-resistant Candida or mixed infections, contributing to poor cure rates and recurrence. A one-size-fits-all approach ignores significant geographical variation in etiology and antimicrobial resistance, wasting resources and driving further resistance. A diagnostic-first strategy is advocated but lacks evidence-based, region-specific thresholds to guide when testing is cost-effective and essential versus when empiric therapy remains justified.
proposals.hypothesis: Synthesizing multinational prevalence and resistance data will reveal statistically significant geographical clusters of vaginitis etiology, enabling the creation of a validated, dynamic algorithm that defines when first-line etiological testing is necessary for optimal patient outcomes compared to empiric therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis of pre-2026 studies reporting microbiologically confirmed prevalence and resistance patterns for vaginitis pathogens across defined global regions. Data will be synthesized using random-effects models to generate pooled estimates with confidence intervals. A decision-tree algorithm will be constructed, integrating prevalence thresholds (e.g., >X% likelihood of azole-resistant Candida), local test availability, and first-line treatment efficacy data from trials (e.g., fluconazole, ibrexafungerp, metronidazole) to output region-specific care pathways. The algorithm's theoretical performance will be validated against existing clinical trial datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, geographically-stratified framework to guide diagnostic and therapeutic decisions for vaginitis, moving the field beyond uniform empiricism. It will offer clear criteria for implementing precision, diagnostic-driven care, potentially improving cure rates and antimicrobial stewardship globally. The resulting dynamic algorithm can be adapted as local epidemiology changes.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Sobel's prior work compares specific drugs to placebo, this proposal innovatively uses large-scale epidemiological synthesis to redefine the initial clinical decision point (test vs. treat). It creates a practical, data-driven tool that translates global variation into actionable local guidelines, a novel application of precision medicine principles to a common gynecologic condition.

proposals.title: Global Prevalence and Etiological Mapping of Vaginitis: A Standardized, Multicenter Epidemiological Study
proposals.motivation: Vaginitis, primarily caused by bacterial vaginosis (BV), vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC), and trichomoniasis, is a major global cause of morbidity and healthcare visits for women. Current prevalence data are fragmented, often region-specific, and lack standardized diagnostic criteria, hindering accurate burden assessment and targeted therapeutic development. A comprehensive global map is urgently needed to inform public health strategy and clinical trial design for new agents like ibrexafungerp.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prevalence and etiological distribution of vaginitis differ significantly across geographic regions and demographic groups, and that a substantial proportion of symptomatic cases, particularly recurrent VVC, are misdiagnosed or undiagnosed under current clinical practices.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, cross-sectional, clinic-based study across 30-40 sites in North America, Europe, Asia, Africa, and South America. We will enroll approximately 15,000 consecutive symptomatic and asymptomatic women attending routine gynecological care. All participants will undergo standardized, centralized microbiological testing (Nugent score for BV, culture/PCR for Candida species and azole resistance, PCR for Trichomonas vaginalis). Data on demographics, symptoms, prior treatments, and partner management will be collected via a unified electronic case report form. Statistical analysis will employ multivariable logistic regression to identify regional and demographic risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, directly comparable dataset on the global prevalence of specific vaginitis pathogens, establishing a contemporary epidemiological baseline. It will identify high-burden regions for targeted intervention and reveal gaps in current diagnostic pathways. The data will directly inform the design of future global therapeutic trials, including for recurrent VVC and azole-resistant Candida.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies are regional or focus on single pathogens, this proposal implements a unified, high-resolution diagnostic protocol across diverse global populations simultaneously, allowing for true comparative epidemiology. It integrates the assessment of azole resistance and partner treatment history, linking epidemiology directly to current therapeutic challenges identified in Dr. Sobel's recent work.

proposals.title: Prevalence, Determinants, and Sequelae of Subclinical Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: A Longitudinal Community-Based Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Clinic-based studies of vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) fundamentally over-represent symptomatic disease, creating selection bias that obscures the true burden and natural history of Candida colonization. The prevalence, host factors, and long-term clinical consequences of subclinical VVC—a potential reservoir for recurrence and transmission—remain poorly defined. A population-representative cohort is essential to accurately quantify this burden and identify determinants of symptomatic versus asymptomatic outcomes, informing targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subclinical vaginal Candida colonization is highly prevalent, is associated with distinct host immunological and microbiome profiles compared to symptomatic infection, and confers a significant risk for progression to symptomatic VVC and long-term reproductive morbidity.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective cohort of 800 reproductive-age women, representative of the community, with no active VVC symptoms at enrollment. Participants will provide self-collected vaginal swabs and detailed behavioral/clinical data quarterly for 24 months. Swabs will undergo quantitative PCR for Candida species (including C. albicans, C. glabrata) and azole resistance markers (e.g., ERG11 mutations), 16S rRNA gene sequencing for bacterial microbiota, and multiplex cytokine analysis. Symptom development triggers an immediate follow-up visit. Longitudinal data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models to identify predictors of state transitions (colonization to symptomatic VVC or clearance).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, population-based estimates of subclinical VVC prevalence and incidence. It will define host immunological and microbiomic correlates of asymptomatic carriage versus disease, offering new targets for intervention. Furthermore, it will directly measure the clinical consequence of subclinical colonization by quantifying its risk for progression to symptomatic VVC, informing the debate on screening and pre-emptive treatment.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond clinic-based case-control designs to a true community cohort, eliminating the key selection bias in VVC research. It integrates longitudinal molecular pathogen characterization with host immune profiling to dynamically model the determinants of infection outcome, a approach not yet applied to fungal vaginitis.

proposals.title: Prevalence, Mycobiota Composition, and Clinical Consequences of Asymptomatic Vulvovaginal Candida Colonization: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) remains a major therapeutic challenge. The role of asymptomatic colonization as a reservoir for subsequent symptomatic episodes is poorly understood, and current treatment paradigms focus solely on symptomatic disease. Defining the epidemiology and mycological characteristics of subclinical carriage is crucial for developing strategies to prevent recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that asymptomatic vaginal Candida colonization, particularly with non-albicans species or strains exhibiting reduced azole susceptibility, is a significant risk factor for the development of future symptomatic acute VVC or RVVC episodes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month prospective cohort study of 300 women with a history of VVC but currently asymptomatic. Participants will undergo quarterly visits with detailed symptom assessment and vaginal sampling for culture, species identification (using CHROMagar and PCR), and antifungal susceptibility testing (CLSI broth microdilution for fluconazole, ibrexafungerp). A nested case-control analysis will compare baseline mycological profiles of women who develop symptomatic VVC during follow-up to matched controls who remain asymptomatic.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective data on the rate of progression from asymptomatic carriage to symptomatic VVC. It will define the mycobiota associated with subclinical states and identify specific microbiological risk factors (e.g., species, MICs) for breakthrough infection, informing future trials of pre-emptive or suppressive therapy in high-risk carriers.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this group and others has focused on treating symptomatic RVVC (e.g., with ibrexafungerp), this proposal shifts the paradigm to the pre-symptomatic phase. It prospectively links colonization microbiology to clinical outcomes, moving beyond cross-sectional prevalence surveys.

proposals.title: Defining Functional Resilience Traits of the Vaginal Microbiome: A Multi-Omics Study to Predict Stability Against Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Current research on vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) and recurrent VVC (RVVC) predominantly focuses on eradicating pathogenic Candida species or suppressing symptomatic flares. This reactive, disturbance-centric model mirrors early antifungal trials like those with fluconazole and ibrexafungerp. A paradigm shift is needed to understand the proactive maintenance factors of vaginal health, which could lead to preventative strategies rather than repeated treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: A healthy vaginal environment possesses identifiable, measurable 'functional resilience traits'—integrating specific microbial metabolic outputs, host epithelial barrier integrity, and immunological factors—that actively maintain ecological stability and resist a shift to Candida overgrowth and symptomatic VVC.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal case-control study of 120 women (60 with RVVC history, 60 healthy controls). At baseline and monthly follow-ups, we will collect vaginal samples for: 1) Metagenomic sequencing to reconstruct microbial community metabolic networks (using tools like HUMAnN3 and MetaCyc), 2) Metabolomic profiling (LC-MS) to quantify antimicrobial peptides (e.g., defensins, SLPI), lactic acid isomers, and other metabolites, and 3) Transcriptomic analysis of epithelial cell scrapings to assess barrier function genes. Ecological network analysis and machine learning (e.g., Random Forest regression) will integrate these multi-omics layers to identify keystone features predictive of stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from a pathogen-focused to a system-resilience framework for vaginal health. It will generate a novel, predictive model of stability factors, identifying potential new biomarkers for susceptibility and targets for probiotic or prebiotic interventions aimed at bolstering innate resistance to dysbiosis.
proposals.novelty: This study is the first to explicitly apply ecological resilience theory from ecosystem science to vaginal health, seeking to define the functional properties that *prevent* infection, rather than just characterizing the state of infection or its direct causes.

proposals.title: A Prospective Cohort Study of Clinical, Microbiomic, and Behavioral Factors Associated with Sustained Vaginal Health and Resistance to Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) research is heavily focused on pathogen eradication and antifungal resistance, with less attention paid to the host factors that maintain a resilient, infection-resistant state. A pre-2026 shift towards understanding the vaginal microbiome's protective role highlights the need to systematically identify the positive correlates of sustained health, not just the triggers of disease. This knowledge is critical for developing non-azole-based maintenance strategies and personalized prevention plans.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that women who remain free of symptomatic VVC for extended periods, despite a history of RVVC, will exhibit a distinct, stable profile of host immune markers, vaginal microbiome composition, and behavioral practices compared to those who experience frequent recurrences.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 12-month prospective cohort study of 200 women with a documented history of RVVC, all initially treated to a healthy baseline. Participants will be stratified into 'Maintainers' (no symptomatic recurrence) and 'Recurrers' (≥2 culture-confirmed episodes). We will collect monthly self-collected vaginal swabs for 16S rRNA and ITS2 sequencing to define bacterial and fungal communities, alongside mucosal cytokine analysis (e.g., IL-1β, IL-22, SLPI). Detailed behavioral and clinical data will be captured via electronic diaries and validated questionnaires. A multivariate Cox proportional hazards model, adjusted for antifungal use, will identify factors independently associated with recurrence-free survival.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, longitudinal profile of the vaginal ecosystem in women who successfully avoid RVVC recurrence. It will identify specific microbial consortia and host immune signatures associated with protection. The findings will directly inform the development of novel probiotic, immunomodulatory, or behavioral interventions aimed at promoting durable vaginal health.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the traditional disease-focused paradigm by prospectively defining the 'healthy resilient state' in a high-risk population. It integrates longitudinal multi-omics with detailed behavioral data to model maintenance, moving beyond the standard case-control comparisons of active infection.

proposals.title: Integrated Network Analysis of Vaginal Mucosal Defense and Candida albicans Pathogenesis in Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) remains a debilitating condition for many women, with treatment often focused on antifungal suppression rather than correcting underlying host susceptibility. Pre-2026 research has cataloged clinical outcomes and isolated microbial factors, but a systems-level understanding of the host-pathogen interface is lacking. Defining the specific molecular networks in the vaginal mucosa that either successfully block or permissively enable Candida infection is critical for moving from reactive treatment to proactive, resilience-based prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: Women with RVVC possess a dysregulated molecular interaction network at the vaginal mucosa, characterized by attenuated defensive host pathways and enhanced engagement of specific Candida albicans virulence programs, compared to women who are colonized but asymptomatic or infection-resistant.
proposals.method: We will perform a longitudinal case-control study of women with RVVC, asymptomatic Candida colonization, and healthy controls, collecting vaginal mucosal samples during health and at infection onset. Samples will undergo dual RNA-seq (host and fungal transcriptomics) and targeted proteomics (host defense peptides, cytokines, fungal enzymes). Data integration and network construction will be performed using established algorithms (e.g., weighted gene co-expression network analysis - WGCNA) and causal inference tools (like the PC algorithm) to model directional host-pathogen interactions. This will map concurrent 'attack' (fungal) and 'defense' (host) subnetworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, integrated interaction map of host defense and C. albicans pathogenesis in the human vaginal mucosa. It will identify critical protective host nodes whose function defines resilience, offering novel biomarkers for susceptibility. The network model will provide a framework for developing host-targeted therapeutic or probiotic strategies aimed at reinforcing these protective pathways to prevent recurrence.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies focusing on either host factors or microbial virulence in isolation, this proposal simultaneously profiles both sides of the interaction within the same biological samples to construct a causal, mechanistic network. This integrated 'cartography' approach is novel for RVVC and moves beyond correlation to infer functional interaction dynamics.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Evaluate the Non-Inferiority of Serum-Based Treatment Decisions versus Lumbar Puncture-Guided Therapy in Ocular Syphilis with High-Titer VDRL
proposals.motivation: Lumbar puncture (LP) for cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis is standard in ocular syphilis to rule out neurosyphilis, but it is invasive, carries risks, and delays treatment. In acute, florid uveitis with very high serum VDRL/RPR titers (e.g., ≥1:128), neurosyphilis is often presumed, yet the necessity of the LP for treatment decisions remains debated. A streamlined, evidence-based approach could reduce patient morbidity and healthcare costs without compromising outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients presenting with acute, florid uveitis and high serum VDRL titers (≥1:128), treatment with a neurosyphilis-level intravenous penicillin regimen, without prior CSF analysis, will be non-inferior to an LP-guided treatment strategy in achieving clinical resolution and serological cure at 6-month follow-up.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Participants with confirmed syphilitic uveitis and serum VDRL ≥1:128 will be randomized 1:1 to either: (1) Arm A: Immediate LP (active procedure) followed by LP-guided therapy, or (2) Arm B: Simulated LP (placebo procedure) with immediate initiation of intravenous aqueous penicillin G (24 million units daily for 14 days). The primary outcome is the proportion of patients achieving clinical resolution of uveitis and a ≥4-fold decrease in serum VDRL titer at 6 months. Non-inferiority will be assessed with a pre-specified margin. Secondary outcomes include visual acuity, time to treatment initiation, and procedure-related adverse events.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence to challenge a deeply ingrained clinical practice, potentially establishing a new standard of care for a specific, high-risk patient subset. It will directly inform guidelines on the necessity of CSF evaluation in ocular syphilis, aiming to reduce unnecessary invasive procedures and expedite definitive therapy.
proposals.novelty: While observational studies have questioned the utility of LP in ocular syphilis, this is the first randomized controlled trial to directly compare a serum-based treatment algorithm against the standard LP-guided approach, applying rigorous methodology from infectious disease and ophthalmology clinical trials to a diagnostic paradigm.

proposals.title: Antibiotic Use as a Surrogate Marker for Host Susceptibility: A Prospective Cohort Study of Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis and Urinary Tract Infection Risk
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic use is a well-established risk factor for vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) and urinary tract infections (UTIs), but it is often treated as a direct, causative disruptor of the microbiome. This paradigm may oversimplify a more complex host-pathogen relationship. If antibiotic prescriptions are instead a marker for underlying immunological, anatomical, or behavioral vulnerabilities, it would fundamentally shift prevention strategies from broad-spectrum prophylaxis to targeted host-directed interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Recent antibiotic use is not an independent, direct cause of recurrent VVC and UTIs, but rather a clinical indicator of other, unmeasured host susceptibility factors that predispose individuals to both infection and the conditions requiring antibiotic therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of 500 women with a history of recurrent VVC, mirroring the design of recent ibrexafungerp trials. Participants will provide detailed medical histories, undergo baseline vaginal microbiome sequencing (16S rRNA and ITS2), and complete serial questionnaires over 12 months of follow-up. We will use multivariate Cox proportional hazards regression to model time-to-next symptomatic VVC or UTI episode, with antibiotic use as a time-dependent covariate. The analysis will rigorously control for confounders (e.g., sexual activity, contraceptive use) and test for interaction effects between antibiotic exposure and baseline host factors (e.g., cytokine profiles, genetic polymorphisms in innate immunity genes).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence to disentangle antibiotic exposure from inherent host risk in recurrent mucosal infections. It will generate a refined risk-prediction model that could identify a high-risk phenotype independent of antibiotic use. The findings could redirect clinical research towards investigating host-targeted therapies (e.g., immunomodulators, biofilm disruptors) rather than solely focusing on antimicrobial or antifungal prophylaxis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes antibiotic use as a risk factor, this study explicitly tests the novel, non-obvious causal direction—that the need for an antibiotic is the consequence of a susceptible phenotype, not the primary cause of subsequent infection. It integrates deep host-factor profiling with longitudinal clinical follow-up, a method not previously applied to this question in the VVC/UTI population.

proposals.title: Characterization of the Urinary Microbiota in Women with Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis: Association with Behavioral Risk Factors and Clinical Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) is a debilitating condition affecting many women, with treatment options like fluconazole facing challenges from resistance. Pre-2026 research has established the gut and vaginal microbiomes as reservoirs for Candida, but the role of the urinary/bladder microbiota in RVVC pathogenesis and recurrence is poorly understood. Defining this niche's microbial ecology and its link to modifiable behavioral factors (e.g., sexual activity, hygiene practices) could reveal novel mechanisms of recurrence and prevention targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that women with RVVC harbor a distinct, dysbiotic urinary microbiota compared to healthy controls, and that specific compositional features of this microbiota are associated with known behavioral risk factors for RVVC and predict clinical response to antifungal therapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of women with RVVC and matched controls, collecting catheterized urine samples at baseline and post-treatment. Microbial DNA will be extracted and the V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene will be amplified and sequenced on an Illumina MiSeq platform. Bioinformatic analysis will use QIIME2 (pre-2026 standard) for demultiplexing, DADA2 for amplicon sequence variant (ASV) calling, and taxonomic assignment via the SILVA database. Associations between microbiota profiles (alpha/beta diversity, specific taxa) and behavioral risk factors (from structured questionnaires) and clinical outcomes will be tested using multivariate statistical models (PERMANOVA, linear/logistic regression).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive characterization of the urinary microbiota in RVVC, establishing its baseline state and dysbiotic patterns. It will directly link microbial signatures to specific behavioral risks, offering a mechanistic explanation for their role in recurrence. The findings could identify novel microbial biomarkers for recurrence risk and inform future interventions targeting the urinary microbiome to prevent RVVC.
proposals.novelty: While the gut and vaginal microbiomes have been studied in candidiasis, applying high-resolution 16S sequencing to systematically profile the urinary microbiota in RVVC is novel. Integrating this microbial data with detailed behavioral risk factor analysis to explain clinical outcomes represents a significant advance beyond descriptive correlative studies.

proposals.title: Development and Clinical Validation of a Point-of-Care PCR Test for Rapid Candida Species Differentiation in Urine Specimens from Women with Suspected Candiduria.
proposals.motivation: Candiduria, particularly in women with recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC), is a common clinical finding, but current culture-based diagnostic methods are slow and often do not speciate. This delay hinders timely, targeted therapy, which is critical given the rising prevalence of azole-resistant non-albicans Candida species (e.g., C. glabrata). A rapid, point-of-care (POC) diagnostic tool would enable immediate species-specific treatment decisions at the clinic visit.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel, miniaturized, isothermal PCR-based POC assay can accurately and rapidly (<30 minutes) differentiate Candida albicans from key non-albicans species (C. glabrata, C. krusei, C. tropicalis) directly from urine specimens, with performance comparable to standard laboratory PCR.
proposals.method: We will adapt loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) technology, proven in pre-2026 virology POC tests, for Candida detection. Species-specific LAMP primer sets will be designed for conserved genomic regions (e.g., ITS2, D1/D2). The assay will be integrated into a simple, disposable microfluidic cartridge for urine processing and nucleic acid amplification, coupled with a portable fluorescence reader. The prototype will be validated against quantitative PCR and culture using a biorepository of urine samples from women with documented candiduria.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical validation of a true POC molecular test for Candida speciation in urine. It directly addresses the unmet need for rapid diagnostics to guide antifungal selection, potentially reducing empirical fluconazole use and improving outcomes in RVVC and complicated urinary tract infections. The platform could serve as a model for adapting other molecular infectious disease diagnostics to POC settings.
proposals.novelty: While PCR-based Candida detection exists in central labs, the novel application lies in miniaturizing and adapting isothermal amplification (LAMP) into a dedicated, user-friendly POC format for urine, a complex matrix, specifically for clinical mycology—a field lagging behind virology in rapid diagnostic translation.

proposals.title: Defining Vaginal Mucosal Immune Endotypes in Women with Recurrent Vulvovaginal Candidiasis Without Classic Risk Factors: A Case-Control Study
proposals.motivation: Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis (RVVC) remains a debilitating condition despite the development of new antifungal agents like ibrexafungerp. The persistent focus on empiric antifungal therapy, while necessary, fails to address the underlying host susceptibilities in many women, leading to high recurrence rates. A paradigm shift towards understanding local host defense failures, akin to advances in recurrent UTI research, is critically needed to develop rational, durable therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: Women with idiopathic RVVC, compared to matched controls, possess discrete, measurable defects in key vaginal mucosal defense components—specifically epithelial barrier integrity, Candida-specific IgA response, and antimicrobial peptide (AMP) expression—which define distinct immunological endotypes of disease.
proposals.method: We will conduct a detailed case-control study of 60 women with idiopathic RVVC and 30 healthy controls. Vaginal lavage and mucosal swabs will be collected during the asymptomatic, non-treatment phase. We will quantify epithelial barrier integrity via transepithelial electrical resistance and tight junction protein (claudin-1, occludin) expression. Host defense will be profiled using multiplex ELISA for Candida albicans-specific IgA/IgG and a panel of AMPs (e.g., HBD-2, SLPI, calprotectin). Unsupervised hierarchical clustering and principal component analysis will be used to identify distinct immune endotypes within the RVVC cohort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, concurrent mapping of multiple vaginal host defense axes in RVVC, moving beyond descriptive Candida colonization studies. It will establish a foundational framework of host-derived endotypes, which can be validated in future trials. This mechanistic understanding is essential for guiding the development of targeted immunomodulatory (e.g., IgA-boosting, AMP-replenishing) or microbiome-restorative interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior RVVC research has focused on pathogen factors or systemic immunity, this proposal systematically investigates local mucosal immunity as the primary determinant of susceptibility. It applies an endotype discovery approach, common in asthma and allergy, to the vaginal microenvironment for the first time, seeking to redefine RVVC by host biology rather than solely by fungal recurrence.

ideas.title: Define host defense failures in recurrent vaginitis to move beyond empiric drug development
ideas.description: This research direction posits that the recurrent vaginitis problem, like recurrent UTI in patients without anatomical defects, stems from specific, yet undefined, host defense failures in the vaginal microenvironment. It challenges the pharmaceutical-centric approach by arguing that without a foundational understanding of these host-pathogen interactions—such as defects in epithelial barrier function, local IgA production, or antimicrobial peptide expression—new drugs will remain empiric and suboptimal. The project would systematically characterize the immunological and microbiological landscape of patients with recurrent vaginitis who lack classic risk factors, aiming to define discrete endotypes of host vulnerability. This precise mechanistic understanding would then guide the rational development of targeted immunomodulatory or microbiome-restorative therapies, rather than continued broad-spectrum antimicrobial development.

ideas.title: Point-of-Care Diagnostic Platforms to Guide Empiric Therapy in Vaginitis
ideas.description: This research direction develops and validates novel point-of-care diagnostic tools specifically designed to triage vaginitis cases at the initial clinical encounter. The tools rapidly differentiate between straightforward cases (e.g., uncomplicated candidiasis) where empiric therapy is sufficient and complex cases (e.g., mixed infections, treatment-resistant organisms) that require comprehensive etiological diagnosis. This approach moves beyond the binary debate by creating a clinical workflow that strategically deploys empiric therapy only when it is likely to succeed, reserving advanced diagnostics for high-risk or ambiguous presentations. The goal is to improve patient outcomes and antimicrobial stewardship by making accurate diagnostic guidance an integral, actionable part of the empiric therapy decision.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Psychosocial Burden of Subclinical Vaginal Dysbiosis
ideas.description: This research direction applies validated psychometric scales (e.g., for distress, sexual function, and general well-being) to populations with laboratory-confirmed, subclinical vaginal infections or dysbiosis (e.g., bacterial vaginosis, candidiasis). By systematically comparing these psychological metrics between asymptomatic carriers, symptomatic patients, and healthy controls, the study aims to determine if subclinical states exert a significant, under-recognized psychosocial toll. This approach challenges the binary symptomatic/asymptomatic paradigm by introducing a quantifiable continuum of suffering linked to microbial presence, potentially redefining clinical thresholds for intervention based on quality-of-life impact alongside pathogen detection.

ideas.title: Multiplexed point-of-care dysbiosis panel using repurposed sensing modalities for syndromic vaginitis diagnosis
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the polymicrobial nature of vaginitis by creating a integrated, multi-analyte point-of-care test. It adapts and combines sensing technologies from virology and bacteriology RDTs—such as lateral flow strips for bacterial enzymes, isothermal amplification for fungal RNA, and paper-based microfluidics for pH and amines—into a single multiplexed cartridge. A validated clinical algorithm interprets the panel's complex output, differentiating between bacterial vaginosis, vulvovaginal candidiasis, trichomoniasis, and non-infectious causes based on established diagnostic criteria (e.g., Amsel's, Nugent). This moves beyond simple pathogen detection to provide a syndromic, etiology-specific diagnosis at the point of care.

ideas.title: Apply metagenomic and culture-independent pathogen discovery to unknown vaginitis syndromes
ideas.description: This research adapts the successful pathogen discovery paradigm from environmental and systemic infectious disease studies to solve the analogous problem of unknown vaginitis etiology. The analogous solution involved using culture-independent metagenomic sequencing and bioinformatic host/subtraction to identify novel pathogens. The adapted idea is to apply this pipeline to well-characterized vaginal samples from patients with idiopathic clinical syndromes, using deep sequencing and sophisticated computational subtraction of all known vaginal microbiome and pathogen sequences. The resulting unassigned genetic material will be assembled, annotated, and screened for novel microbial genomes, enabling the discovery of previously unrecognized fastidious, viral, or unculturable organisms that could be causative agents, thereby elucidating the etiology of currently unexplained vaginitis cases.

ideas.title: Use in vitro tissue models for pathogenesis
ideas.description: Apply advanced cell culture and tissue engineering methods to study vaginal infection mechanisms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Vaginal infections in adult women.

ideas.title: Deploying multiplexed point-of-care diagnostics as discovery tools for unknown etiologies in common vaginitis
ideas.description: This research direction proposes adapting advanced, multiplexed point-of-care diagnostic platforms (e.g., CRISPR-based, multiplex PCR, or antigen arrays from virology) to comprehensively profile the vaginal microenvironment in patients with common vaginitis symptoms. Crucially, it inverts their typical use from mere confirmation of known pathogens to a discovery tool, specifically targeting cases where standard diagnostic workflows are negative. By systematically analyzing these 'idiopathic' cases with high-resolution, multi-analyte tests, the project aims to identify novel microbial, host protein, or metabolic biomarkers, challenging the assumption that the etiology of highly prevalent vaginitis is fully understood. This synergy turns a diagnostic tool into an etiological research instrument for a common but potentially incompletely characterized condition.

ideas.title: Linking Pathogen Discovery to Suffering: A Molecular Epidemiology of Vaginitis-Associated Morbidity
ideas.description: This research program applies advanced molecular epidemiology not just to identify unknown pathogens in vaginitis, but to rigorously quantify their specific contribution to the profound physical, psychological, and social suffering historically dismissed as a 'nuisance.' By correlating deep genomic sequencing data from vaginal microbiota with detailed, patient-reported outcomes on pain, sexual function, mental health, and quality of life, it seeks to establish causal microbial drivers of morbidity. This shifts the paradigm from merely cataloguing microbes to defining 'high-burden' pathogens, thereby creating an evidence base to prioritize diagnostic development and therapeutic research for the microbes that cause the most significant human suffering.

ideas.title: CSF evaluation may be unnecessary in acute cases
ideas.description: Challenge the need for CSF tap in acute, florid uveitis if serum VDRL is high and neurosyphilis is already presumed.
ideas.sourceTitle: Syphilitic posterior uveitis: correlative findings and significance.

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse: factors preventing resistance

ideas.sourceTitle: Current treatment options for vulvovaginal candidiasis caused by azole-resistant Candida species

ideas.title: Molecular cartography of the vaginal mucosal barrier: Decoding protective and permissive host-pathogen interaction networks
ideas.description: This research direction moves beyond cataloging clinical outcomes or isolated virulence factors to construct a high-resolution, molecular interaction network of the vaginal mucosa. It simultaneously maps the detailed pathogenic pathways used by specific microbes (e.g., bacterial vaginosis-associated bacteria, Candida, STIs) and the constitutive and inducible host molecular programs that actively block these pathways to maintain health. By comparing these 'attack' and 'defense' networks within the same molecular framework, the work will identify critical protective nodes whose function defines resilience and whose dysregulation creates permissive states for infection. This integrated map will enable a shift from reactive treatment to proactive, personalized strategies aimed at reinforcing identified protective networks to prevent pathogenesis before it begins.

ideas.title: Study subclinical or asymptomatic infections

ideas.sourceTitle: Vaginal infections in adult women.

ideas.title: The High Stakes of Incomplete Knowledge: Why Drug-Centric Approaches to Vaginitis Perpetuate Suffering
ideas.description: This fused idea argues that the prevailing pharmaceutical-centric model for treating vaginitis is fundamentally inadequate because it addresses symptoms without resolving complex, often recurrent underlying causes. By framing vaginitis as a major source of chronic physical discomfort, psychological distress, and socioeconomic burden, the approach highlights the profound human cost of this clinical neglect. The synergy reveals that the lack of investment in deep etiological and pathogenic understanding is the key bottleneck, trapping patients in a cycle of temporary drug-based relief and recurrent suffering. Therefore, the research direction calls for a paradigm shift from drug development in isolation to a comprehensive, mechanism-based research program aimed at true disease interception and prevention, justified by the scale of unmet need.

ideas.title: Scale up epidemiological studies globally
ideas.description: Conduct large-scale, multinational prevalence studies to map the global burden of vaginitis causes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Vaginal infections in adult women.

ideas.title: Vaginitis as a Chronic Inflammatory Disease: Reclassifying from Nuisance to Systemic Burden
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that vaginitis is trivial by drawing an analogy from gastroenterology's successful reclassification of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD). IBD was once dismissed as a minor functional issue but was reframed as a serious chronic inflammatory disease through research quantifying its multidimensional burden, systemic effects, and long-term risks. Adapting this solution, the research would systematically map the full burden of vaginitis (pain, psychological, economic), investigate its inflammatory profile and systemic connections, and study long-term mucosal health impacts. This approach aims to shift the paradigm from viewing vaginitis as a mere 'nuisance' infection to recognizing it as a significant condition of inflammatory dysregulation with serious consequences for women's health.

ideas.title: Co-Designed, Implementation-Ready Diagnostic Systems for Syndromic Vaginitis Management
ideas.description: This research program develops and validates novel diagnostic tools for vaginitis through a parallel, integrated focus on technical performance and real-world implementation. It engages clinicians, laboratory staff, and patients in the design process to ensure the tools meet practical needs for speed, cost, and simplicity. The final output is a validated diagnostic system paired with a tailored implementation toolkit, including guidelines and cost-benefit analyses, to ensure rapid adoption and a definitive move beyond empiric therapy across diverse healthcare settings.

ideas.title: Ecological Network-Based Diagnostic Platform for Vaginal Dysbiosis
ideas.description: This research fuses ecological community analysis with diagnostic development to create a novel platform that diagnoses vaginitis by analyzing the structure, stability, and functional interactions within the vaginal microbiome network. It uses metrics like community resilience, keystone species identification, and metabolic pathway disruption to generate an 'Ecological Health Index' that distinguishes between true pathogenic invasion, simple dysbiosis, and compensatory states. This enables precise, mechanism-based diagnoses that guide targeted interventions—such as specific probiotics or prebiotics to restore ecological balance—rather than relying on broad-spectrum empiric therapy, ultimately improving treatment outcomes and reducing recurrence.

ideas.title: Precision Diagnosis as a Mandate for Dignity: Ending the Suffering of Undifferentiated Vaginitis
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the dual status quo of dismissing vaginitis as trivial while simultaneously managing it with imprecise empiric therapy. It posits that the widespread suffering associated with vaginitis is perpetuated and amplified by the diagnostic inaccuracy of empiric approaches, which lead to treatment failures, recurrent symptoms, and patient frustration. Therefore, the project advocates for making rapid, accessible, and precise etiological diagnosis the new standard of care, framing it as an essential, dignity-restoring intervention. The core hypothesis is that shifting to a 'diagnosis-first' paradigm will directly and measurably reduce the multidimensional burden of suffering—physical, psychological, and social—currently borne by patients.

ideas.title: Assume antibiotic use is a consequence, not cause
ideas.description: Study if recent antibiotic use is a marker for other hidden vulnerabilities rather than a direct UTI risk factor.

ideas.title: A dynamic vaginal mucosa-on-a-chip model integrating mechanical cues and resident microbiome for studying infection pathogenesis
ideas.description: This adversarially improved research system employs a microfluidic organ-chip device lined with a differentiated, stratified vaginal epithelium and underlying stromal fibroblasts. The chip incorporates cyclic mechanical strain to mimic physiological distension and a perfused lumen to establish chemical gradients and enable the stable co-culture of a defined consortium of commensal bacteria. This setup allows for the real-time, multimodal investigation of infection mechanisms by bacterial vaginosis-associated pathogens or Candida species within a context that includes critical host mechanical signals and microbiome-pathogen interactions, thereby generating more clinically relevant insights into disease initiation and progression.

ideas.title: Prospective community cohort study to define the prevalence, determinants, and longitudinal outcomes of subclinical infection
ideas.description: This research establishes a longitudinal, population-representative cohort actively screened for specific pathogens via regular biospecimen collection, irrespective of symptoms, to accurately quantify the burden of subclinical infection. It integrates detailed virological characterization, host immune profiling, and comprehensive health and behavioral data to identify factors that determine symptomatic versus asymptomatic outcomes. The design enables direct assessment of consequences, including duration of shedding, transmission potential, and long-term health sequelae, for both infection states. This approach overcomes the fundamental selection bias of clinic-based studies to provide robust estimates of the true scale and impact of asymptomatic carriage.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Systemic Burden of Vaginitis: A Composite Metric for Health Policy
ideas.description: This research moves beyond qualitative claims to develop and validate a standardized, multi-dimensional 'Vaginitis Burden Score' (VBS). The VBS integrates four objective domains: prevalence/incidence data, direct treatment costs, indirect costs from lost productivity and caregiving, and quality-adjusted life year (QALY) decrements measured using validated instruments. By benchmarking the VBS against established burden metrics for other chronic conditions (e.g., migraine, asthma), the project provides policymakers and health systems with a rigorous, comparable tool to demonstrate vaginitis's significant population-level impact, thereby prioritizing it for research funding and clinical guideline development.

ideas.title: A Mandatory Translational Loop: Coupling All New Vaginitis Drug Trials to Parallel Etiology Research
ideas.description: This redesigned idea proposes a structural solution that forces the integration of pathogenesis research and drug development. It mandates that any clinical trial for a new vaginitis therapeutic must include a funded, parallel research cohort undergoing detailed molecular and microbiological profiling to elucidate etiology. The data from this cohort is used to refine disease subtypes and mechanisms in real-time, creating a feedback loop where drug efficacy is understood in context. This system ensures that pharmaceutical progress directly generates and is guided by a deeper biological understanding, moving beyond critique to an enforceable model for sustainable advancement.

ideas.title: Dual: Study vaginal health maintenance factors via ecological resilience theory
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology and ecosystem stability, where scientists study the factors that confer resilience against disturbances, not just the disturbances themselves. The analogous solution involves identifying keystone species and functional traits that maintain ecosystem stability. Adapted to vaginal health, the study will investigate the dual problem by defining the 'functional resilience traits' of a healthy vaginal environment. This involves a multi-omics approach to characterize the microbial metabolic networks, host-derived antimicrobial peptides, and epithelial barrier properties that actively prevent a shift to dysbiosis, thereby providing a predictive framework for maintenance factors rather than just causal factors for infection.

ideas.title: Global Etiological Mapping to Drive Diagnostic-First Precision Therapy
ideas.description: This research synthesizes large-scale multinational prevalence data with a diagnostic-first philosophy to create a dynamic, geographically-stratified diagnostic and treatment algorithm. By mapping the precise prevalence and antimicrobial resistance patterns of vaginitis pathogens (bacterial, fungal, protozoal) across diverse global regions, it generates evidence to define when empiric therapy is statistically justified versus when first-line etiological testing is essential for optimal outcomes. The resulting framework empowers clinicians to move beyond one-size-fits-all empiricism to implement precision, diagnostic-driven care pathways tailored to local epidemiological realities, thereby improving cure rates and slowing antimicrobial resistance.

ideas.title: Integrated Diagnostic-Therapeutic Bundles for Acute Management
ideas.description: This approach reframes the diagnosis-versus-therapy dichotomy by embedding rapid, culture-independent molecular diagnostics (e.g., multiplex PCR, mass spectrometry) into the initial clinical response protocol for acute syndromes like sepsis, meningitis, or pneumonia. Empiric therapy is initiated immediately per standard guidelines, but the concurrent diagnostic assay runs in parallel, with results available within 1-2 hours to guide definitive therapy. The system mandates a structured 'bundle' where the initial antibiotic order includes a hard stop for review and adjustment based on the diagnostic result, ensuring empiric therapy is not an endpoint but a bridge to precision treatment.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Health Indices to Quantify Vaginitis Impact
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists quantify ecosystem degradation using composite indices like the Index of Biotic Integrity, this idea adapts that methodological framework to measure the impact of vaginitis. In ecology, disparate biological metrics are standardized, weighted, and combined into a single score reflecting overall ecosystem health. The adapted solution is to create a Vaginitis Impact Composite Index (VICI) by selecting and integrating relevant sub-scores from validated quality-of-life, pain, distress, and functional impairment scales used in psychology and gynecology. This produces a unified, quantifiable metric of patient suffering, enabling clearer severity staging, better tracking of treatment efficacy over time, and more robust comparison of impact across different etiologies of vaginitis.

ideas.title: Establish a Global Vaginitis Etiology and Resistance Sentinel Surveillance Network
ideas.description: This redesigned system creates a permanent network of sentinel clinical sites across diverse epidemiological zones to perform continuous, standardized surveillance of vaginitis causes and antimicrobial resistance patterns. It employs harmonized diagnostic protocols and integrates point-of-care test data with a secure, centralized analytics platform. This enables real-time, dynamic mapping of disease burden, tracking of emerging pathogens or resistance, and assessment of intervention impacts. The system is designed to provide actionable, timely data for public health response rather than a static, one-time prevalence snapshot.

ideas.title: Etiology-Stratified Longitudinal Mapping of Multidimensional Distress and Care Pathways in Vaginitis
ideas.description: This research establishes a longitudinal cohort study to quantify the multidimensional burden of vaginitis by stratifying participants based on specific etiology (e.g., BV, VVC, AV), symptom profile, and time to diagnosis. It integrates standardized quality-of-life questionnaires with smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to capture real-time fluctuations in psychological and social distress in daily life. Crucially, the study concurrently tracks clinical management pathways, treatment outcomes, and healthcare utilization, enabling analysis of how specific diagnostic delays, treatment failures, or clinician interactions directly exacerbate or alleviate patient suffering. The goal is to produce an actionable model that identifies high-impact intervention points within the clinical care journey to reduce the overall burden of disease.

ideas.title: Diagnostic-Guided Host Defense Modulation
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the sequential model of 'diagnose then treat with antimicrobials' by proposing that an accurate etiological diagnosis should directly inform a host-directed therapeutic strategy. It moves beyond empiric therapy by using diagnostic data to design interventions—such as targeted immunomodulators, microbiome manipulation, or trained immunity protocols—that enhance the host's dynamic defense system specifically against the identified threat. This transforms diagnosis from a tool for selecting a passive drug into a blueprint for actively engineering a more resilient host state, potentially reducing reliance on broad-spectrum antimicrobials and improving long-term outcomes.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution Framework for Antimicrobial Failure (CAFAF): Disentangling Host, Drug, and Pathogen Drivers
ideas.description: This system transforms the ill-posed inverse problem into a structured causal inference pipeline for analyzing antimicrobial therapy failure. It first rigorously excludes host-related (e.g., pharmacokinetic, immune) and treatment-related (e.g., adherence, dosing) confounders using clinical data and therapeutic drug monitoring. Subsequently, it applies deep metagenomic sequencing and transcriptomic profiling of the infection site to detect cryptic or resistant pathogens, followed by in vitro pathogenicity validation in relevant infection models. The output is a probabilistically ranked set of attributable causes, enabling the distinction between true novel resistance, host compromise, and diagnostic error.

ideas.title: Apply a Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS) framework to identify host susceptibility loci for polymicrobial infections
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from complex trait genetics, specifically Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS), to the problem of dissecting host factors in infection. The analogous solution in plant and human genetics uses genome-wide genotyping of large populations to statistically link genetic variants to disease susceptibility or other complex traits without prior mechanistic bias. Adapted to this context, the research would employ advanced molecular-genetic methods (e.g., SNP arrays, whole-genome sequencing) on a large cohort of patients with well-characterized polymicrobial infections. By performing a GWAS that correlates host genetic data with precise clinical phenotypes—such as the microbial consortium composition, infection persistence, and tissue damage severity—the study would identify host susceptibility loci that specifically influence the outcome of complex, multi-pathogen interactions, mirroring how bacterial virulence factors are mapped but from the host genomic perspective.

ideas.title: Precision Diagnostics via Rapid Phenotypic Screening
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that empiric therapy is sufficient for diagnosis by proposing a shift to rapid, precise etiological identification first, adapted from environmental microbiology. In bioremediation, the analogous problem of treating contaminated sites without knowing the specific microbial players was solved by using rapid phenotypic screening assays on-site to guide targeted interventions. Adapted to clinical medicine, this becomes a research program to develop a high-throughput phenotypic diagnostic platform: a patient sample is exposed to a miniaturized array of micro-environments (varying nutrients, stresses, and antimicrobial candidates) to generate a precise functional fingerprint of the infecting pathogen(s) within a clinically relevant timeframe. Treatment is then selected based on this direct phenotypic profile, moving beyond reliance on empiric therapy driven by statistical likelihoods and reducing antimicrobial misuse and diagnostic delay.

ideas.title: Applying Dark Matter Detection Methods to Uncover Rare or Unknown Infectious Etiologies
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that all infections are caused by one of the three common pathogen types (bacterial, viral, fungal) by adapting a solution from astrophysics. In cosmology, dark matter's existence was proven through indirect detection by analyzing its effects on visible structures. Similarly, for infections of unknown origin, we propose a research program that systematically catalogs and analyzes the indirect 'signatures' of disease—including specific immune cell profiles, cytokine patterns, histopathological findings, and unique epidemiological clusters. By building a detailed profile of the 'shadow' cast by the unknown agent, this method can guide the development of novel diagnostic tools and targeted metagenomic searches to identify rare, novel, or non-canonical infectious causes that evade standard diagnostic frameworks.

ideas.title: Adapting Coral Reef Resilience Metrics for Vaginal Microbiome Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts ecological resilience analysis methods from coral reef science to study the vaginal microbiome. In marine ecology, the health and stability of coral reef communities are assessed using metrics like species richness, evenness, functional redundancy, and the identification of keystone species to model resilience against disturbances and predict tipping points between alternative stable states (e.g., coral-dominated vs. algae-dominated). This solution is adapted by applying the same conceptual framework to longitudinal vaginal microbiome data. The research would involve quantifying analogous stability metrics for microbial communities, identifying keystone bacterial taxa, and modeling ecological tipping points to predict transitions between healthy (e.g., Lactobacillus crispatus-dominated) and dysbiotic (e.g., high-diversity anaerobic) states, thereby creating predictive tools for disease risk and intervention timing.

ideas.title: Psychometric Profiling to Expose Etiological Gaps in High-Prevalence Conditions
ideas.description: This research direction proposes applying a battery of validated psychometric scales (e.g., for pain, distress, sexual function, daily life interference) to a large cohort of patients with a common, yet poorly understood condition like chronic vaginitis. By clustering patients based on their multidimensional suffering profiles rather than traditional clinical signs, the aim is to reveal distinct phenotypic subgroups that current medicine treats as a monolith. These empirically derived subgroups then become the focus for targeted biomedical investigation, hypothesizing that each profile may correlate with a unique, yet currently unknown, etiology or pathophysiological mechanism. Thus, quantified suffering becomes a primary tool for deconstructing a high-prevalence condition into its likely constituent diseases.

ideas.title: Dynamic Causal Taxonomy and Discovery Pipeline for Infection Etiology
ideas.description: This research system establishes a formal, expandable framework for classifying and discovering infectious disease causes. It employs a multi-layered causal model that integrates pathogen, host, and environmental dimensions, moving beyond a fixed list. A dedicated discovery pipeline uses high-throughput 'agnostic' diagnostics and computational causal inference to systematically investigate cases of unknown etiology. The system includes explicit, evidence-based thresholds for incorporating new pathogens or mechanisms into the core taxonomy, ensuring it evolves with new data.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Models to UTI Pathogen-Host Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that recurrent and sporadic UTIs arise from fundamentally different host-pathogen interaction failures by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, the classic distinction between primary and secondary succession was refined by showing that the legacy of initial colonizers can dictate long-term community development, regardless of disturbance. The analogous solution involved detailed longitudinal tracking and network modeling of species interactions. Adapted to UTI research, this suggests testing whether recurrent UTIs are not a distinct etiology but rather the consequence of a specific, persistent initial host-pathogen-microbiome state established during a 'sporadic' infection. The research would employ high-resolution, longitudinal metagenomic and host-response profiling to compare the successional trajectories following UTI onset, searching for a conserved foundational dysbiosis or pathogen reservoir that predicts recurrence, thereby unifying the etiological model.

ideas.title: Etiological Anomaly Detection: A Systematic Framework to Identify and Investigate Mismatches Between Prevalence and Pathophysiological Understanding
ideas.description: This research program establishes a formal methodology to move from a general critique to specific, testable hypotheses. It first defines quantitative metrics—such as treatment resistance rates, heterogeneity in clinical progression, and gaps between diagnostic biomarkers and disease mechanisms—to score the 'explanatory gap' for common conditions. Conditions with high prevalence but high anomaly scores are then prioritized for deep molecular profiling and network analysis, focusing resources on the most likely points of fundamental etiological misunderstanding. The output is a ranked portfolio of high-prevalence conditions with specific, data-supported anomalies, directing future research toward resolving concrete, evidence-based contradictions.

The adapted research idea involves establishing a prospective cohort to systematically collect granular behavioral data (hygiene practices, fluid intake, etc.) alongside UTI outcomes.
