ideas.description: This project moves beyond unified molecular catalogs to build predictive, dynamic models of infection. It employs spatial multi-omics to map the precise biogeography of bacterial virulence factor expression, host immune cell activity, and metabolite gradients within actual infected tissue sections. These empirical maps seed an agent-based computational framework that simulates the probabilistic interactions between host cells, pathogens, and commensals in a spatially explicit environment. The resulting system can test how perturbations—like antibiotic diffusion or immune cell recruitment—alter the local balance of defense and virulence, providing a powerful tool for understanding infection progression and treatment failure.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic 'Silent Earthquake' Detection to Map Asymptomatic Infection Reservoirs
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from geophysics for detecting 'silent earthquakes'—tectonic movements that release energy without traditional seismic signals—to the problem of mapping subclinical and asymptomatic infections. In seismology, the analogous problem was solved by deploying dense networks of sensitive instruments and developing signal processing to capture continuous, low-amplitude data, which was then correlated with geological structures. Adapted to infectious disease, the idea is to implement similarly dense, longitudinal molecular and immunological surveillance in cohort studies to capture the continuous, low-level signal of asymptomatic pathogen carriage and shedding. By applying advanced computational models to this data, correlating it with host factors (genetics, immune status, microbiome) and pathogen genomics, the research aims to map the structure and dynamics of hidden infection reservoirs. This will reveal how these 'silent' transmissions scale down from symptomatic disease, quantify their contribution to population-level pathogen persistence, and predict their consequences for outbreak emergence and intervention efficacy.

ideas.title: Molecular Pathway Circuit Mapping for Host-Pathogen Interaction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the circuit mapping methodology from semiconductor electronics to solve the problem of shifting from clinical syndrome description to detailed molecular pathways in host-pathogen interaction. In electronics, complex systems are decomposed into modular functional blocks (like amplifiers or filters) and their interconnections are mapped to understand signal flow and enable precise intervention. The adapted research idea applies this by modeling a pathogenic infection not as a disease phenotype but as a dysregulated molecular circuit within the host. The goal is to identify and characterize the core functional modules of this circuit—such as pathogen sensor complexes, intracellular signaling cascades, transcriptional regulators, and effector mechanisms—and map their precise connectivity and dynamic signal flow. This 'molecular circuit diagram' would provide a mechanistic blueprint for pathogenesis, moving beyond descriptive associations to a predictive, engineering-like understanding. This enables the development of diagnostics that detect specific circuit malfunctions and therapeutics designed to modulate or repair critical nodes within the pathway, analogous to how a circuit engineer would diagnose and fix a faulty electronic system.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Dynamic Range and Limits of Host Defense Trainability
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the qualitative assertion that host defense is dynamic to rigorously map the boundaries and mechanisms of its plasticity. Using a model host system, it applies controlled training protocols and challenges to measure the capacity for improvement (or deterioration) across distinct defense axes. The output is a quantitative profile of defense trainability, identifying which components are malleable, their associated fitness trade-offs, and the molecular pathways enabling plasticity. This transforms the concept into a falsifiable systems-biology framework that can predict when and how host defenses can be dynamically enhanced.

ideas.title: Genetic modifiers of host molecular pathways determine behavioral risk efficacy
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the traditional model where behavior influences disease risk, which is then modulated by genetics. Instead, it posits that an individual's genetic makeup first determines the molecular configuration of key host-pathogen interaction pathways (e.g., viral entry, innate immune sensing, autophagy). This molecular setup then directly governs the biological potency and clinical impact of a behavioral risk factor. The approach shifts the question from 'How does behavior affect risk?' to 'How do genetic variants determine the degree to which a behavior can pathologically engage a specific molecular pathway?' This enables precise, mechanism-based stratification of behavioral interventions.

ideas.title: Dynamic Interplay of Genetic Propensity and Behavioral Exposure Across Context and Time
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond a static hierarchy to model the dynamic, reciprocal interplay between genetic risk and behavioral factors. It uses a longitudinal design with repeated measures to test how genetic propensity modifies the effect of behavioral exposures on health outcomes, and simultaneously, how behavioral patterns can amplify or mitigate the expression of genetic risk across different life stages and environmental contexts. The core analytical framework integrates polygenic scores, causal inference methods, and time-varying exposure data to map the conditions under which each class of factor serves as the primary modifier of the other.

ideas.title: Integrating Population-Scale Functional Genomics and Systems Genetics to Decode the Polygenic Architecture of Host Susceptibility
ideas.description: This redesigned approach systematically maps host susceptibility factors by combining high-throughput genetic perturbation in primary human cells from diverse donors with population genetic data. It employs pooled CRISPR screens in relevant immune cell types challenged with pathogens to generate a comprehensive atlas of cell-intrinsic host factors. These functional data are then integrated with human GWAS and electronic health record data using computational models to distinguish core protective genes from background genetic noise and to construct predictive, polygenic susceptibility networks. The final output is a validated, hierarchical model of host defense genetics that accounts for allele frequency, effect size, and genetic context, directly addressing the complexity that doomed simpler dissection methods.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Network Analysis to Common Diseases
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that common conditions are well-understood by proposing they may have unknown, systems-level etiologies. It draws an analogy from ecology, where the solution to understanding prevalent phenomena (like algal blooms) shifted from seeking a single cause to employing network analysis of complex, multi-factor interactions. Adapted to medicine, the research idea is to apply similar computational network modeling to high-prevalence diseases. By mapping the dynamic interplay of genetic, microbial, metabolic, and environmental factors, the goal is to reveal the emergent, non-linear drivers that sustain these common conditions, moving beyond simplistic, single-mechanism explanations to uncover novel diagnostic and therapeutic targets.

ideas.title: Apply Crop Resilience GWAS Framework to UTI Susceptibility
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodological framework from GWAS for complex traits in agricultural plant genetics, specifically studies of crop resilience to environmental stressors like pathogens. In that field, solutions involve phenotyping diverse populations under defined stress, conducting GWAS to pinpoint associated genetic loci, and performing functional validation in model systems. Adapted to UTI research, this means applying a similarly rigorous, multi-stage GWAS pipeline: first, deep phenotyping of a human cohort for UTI susceptibility; second, genome-wide analysis to discover host genetic variants; and third, direct functional validation of leading candidate genes in relevant infection models to move from association to mechanistic insight.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune Training: A Neuroplasticity-Inspired Framework
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that host defense is a fixed trait by proposing it is a dynamic, trainable system. The analogy is drawn from computational neuroscience and machine learning, where a neural network's function is not static but is dynamically shaped through iterative training and feedback (e.g., backpropagation). Adapting this solution, the research would conceptualize the immune system as a plastic network. It would investigate if controlled, subclinical immune challenges can serve as 'training data,' leading to measurable, feedback-driven adjustments in immune parameters—such as epigenetic reprogramming of innate immune cells (trained immunity) and altered progenitor cell bias—thereby demonstrating that host defense can be actively 'trained' for enhanced future performance, much like a machine learning model.

ideas.title: Genetic Architecture as a Modifier of Behavioral Risk Pathways
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard public health hierarchy by proposing that genetic mechanisms are not just another risk factor but are primary modifiers of behavioral risk pathways. Drawing an analogy from ecology's 'driver vs. passenger' models in cancer evolution, where genomic context modifies the effect of key drivers, we adapt this to behavioral genetics. The adapted research idea treats behaviors as potential 'drivers' of disease but posits that the individual's genetic architecture sets the context, modifying whether, and how severely, a behavioral risk factor translates to pathology. This would be tested using interaction-focused models in biobank data, examining if polygenic scores and regulatory variants significantly alter the slopes of associations between behaviors (e.g., smoking, exercise) and outcomes like cardiovascular disease or depression.
